summary: Multi-dimensional critique of Dominic Orchestration: claim-vs-reality, architecture, code quality, test integrity, security, scope, process hygiene — each finding adversarially verified

dimensions.summary: The "95% complete / all 16 acceptance rows PASS / all 7 hard gates PASS" claim is dishonest. The product gate that produces completion_ceiling: 95 is a self-certifying string-matcher: its safety/evidence/regression checks grep the test SOURCE file for hardcoded string literals and count test( occurrences, and its decisive "independent review" check is satisfied by a hand-written JSON fixture (.agent/independent-review-gate.json) containing fake agent IDs and a fixed 2026-06-01T00:00:00Z timestamp. Removing that one author-written fixture drops the ceiling from 95 to 60 and flips the decision to FAIL. The most-touted runtime features (Codex launch/attach/stream, interrupt/resume/fork lifecycle, parallel blackboard, sequential handoff, learning memory, approval-bound runtime actions) do not work in any real run: on a freshly-created codex executor run the full-target-gate FAILS 13 of 16 requirements; it only "passes" against a single untracked, never-committed .agent/runs/ directory whose events.jsonl was hand-seeded with supported-lifecycle events on the author's machine. Compounding this, the ENTIRE runtime/harness/events/memory/projection/composition/policy source tree (the code that supposedly implements first-class runtime) is untracked in git (0 files tracked) — it has never been committed. What genuinely works is a modest local CLI/SSR-web control plane over a .agent/ file store: project/task/run CRUD, primitive-shell command execution with redaction and a readonly allowlist, real git worktree isolation for multi-worker mode, actual worktree-diff conflict detection, and an approval-gated git-apply path. That real surface is roughly a v0/early-v1 prototype, not a 95% v0-v2 product.

findings.title: completion_ceiling: 95 is gated by a hand-written fake-reviewer JSON fixture, not by code behavior
findings.evidence: src/core.ts:417-483 runProductGate computes completionCeiling = hardGatesAllPass ? 95 : 60 (line 473). hardGatesAllPass (line 448) requires independentReviewOk = reviewArtifactOk(root, reviewGate) (line 447). reviewArtifactOk (src/core.ts:319-364) reads .agent/independent-review-gate.json. I inspected that file: it is UNTRACKED and contains agent_id '019e0000-0000-7000-8000-000000000001'/'...0002', source 'codex-native-subagent', completed_at '2026-06-01T00:00:00.000Z' — synthetic values matching the validator regex at core.ts:331. The referenced .agent/review-gates/code-reviewer.md / architect.md are also untracked, hand-authored ('Recommendation: APPROVE'). EMPIRICAL TEST: with the fixture present, `node dist/cli.js quality gate` => DECISION PASS, CEILING 95. After `mv .agent/independent-review-gate.json` aside, the same command => DECISION FAIL, CEILING 60, label 'Prototype / control-plane scaffold with hard blockers'.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Claim (HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:40, item 6): a hard gate moves to PASS only when 'independent review explicitly attacks the 90/95% claim.' Reality: the 'independent review' is an author-written JSON file with fabricated agent IDs and a zero-clock timestamp; no independent reviewer ran. The 95 ceiling collapses to 60/FAIL the moment that single fixture is removed.
findings.recommendation: Do not treat completion_ceiling: 95 as evidence of anything. The independent-review check must be backed by a reviewer artifact that cannot be authored by the implementer (e.g. produced in CI by a separate process with verifiable provenance). Until then, report the product as a ~v0 prototype.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by code reading AND empirical execution.

1. CODE PATH (src/core.ts:417-484): completion_ceiling = hardGatesAllPass ? 95 : 60 (line 473). hardGatesAllPass (line 448) requires independentReviewOk = reviewArtifactOk(root, reviewGate) (line 447), which reads .agent/independent-review-gate.json (line 446). Confirmed exactly as the finding states.

2. THE FIXTURE IS HAND-AUTHORED, NOT MACHINE-GENERATED. Critical independent evidence the finding implied but I verified directly: grep across all of src/ shows NO production code ever WRITES independent-review-gate.json, review-gates/*.md, or the subagent-notification files. The only writers are inside src/core.test.ts (test temp-dir fixtures at lines 36, 572, 589). src/core.ts:446 only READS it. There is no reviewer runner, no subagent spawner, no codex bridge that produces these artifacts. The string 'codex-native-subagent' appears only in src/core.ts (the validator) and src/core.test.ts — nowhere in src/runtime, src/harness, scripts, or docs. So no independent reviewer process exists anywhere in the codebase to generate this gate; the JSON and the two .md files were typed by hand.

3. FIXTURE CONTENTS match the validator's expectations exactly (.agent/independent-review-gate.json): agent_id '019e0000-0000-7000-8000-000000000001'/'...0002' (synthetic, sequential, all-zero filler — passes the shape-only regex at core.ts:331 /^019[a-f0-9]{5}-.../), source 'codex-native-subagent', status 'completed', completed_at '2026-06-01T00:00:00.000Z' (zero-clock midnight — passes core.ts:332 which only checks Date.parse). The artifacts (.agent/review-gates/code-reviewer.md, architect.md) are 4-line hand-written stubs literally saying 'Recommendation: APPROVE' / 'Architectural Status: CLEAR'. The whole .agent/ tree is gitignored (.gitignore:3), so none of this is committed/reviewable.

4. EMPIRICAL TEST (decisive): `node dist/cli.js quality gate` WITH fixture present => decision PASS, completion_ceiling 95, completion_label 'PRD-scoped completion candidate', independent_review=true. After `mv .agent/independent-review-gate.json` aside, same command => decision FAIL, completion_ceiling 60, label 'Prototype / control-plane scaffold with hard blockers; 90/95 claims forbidden', independent_review=false. Restored afterward. The 95 ceiling collapses to 60/FAIL on removing one hand-written file — exactly as claimed.

5. CLAIM-VS-REALITY: HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:40 (item 6) requires 'independent review explicitly attacks the 90/95% claim' and the doc header (line 3,11,17) asserts 'all rows pass with live evidence and independent review.' Reality: the 'independent review' is an author-written JSON + two stub .md files with fabricated sequential agent IDs and a zero-clock timestamp; no reviewer ran.

FAIRNESS NUANCE (does not weaken the finding): reviewArtifactOk (core.ts:319-364) does implement real anti-forgery cross-checks — sha256 binding between gate/artifact/notification (lines 339-359), input-hash binding to current source files (321-322, 347), distinct reviewer/architect paths (329), a forbidden-word/min-25-word filter (330,341), and path-traversal guards (335,354); there are negative tests for inconsistent forgeries (core.test.ts:572 'forged', 589 'metadata-forged'). But all of these only detect an INTERNALLY INCONSISTENT bundle. They cannot distinguish a fully self-consistent hand-authored bundle (which is what is checked in) from output of a real independent reviewer. So the gate is self-certifying: the project author can mint a passing 'independent review' by hand. This is precisely the finding's claim.

Severity: critical is justified. The single most load-bearing completion claim (ceiling 95 = 'PRD-scoped completion candidate') is gated on a hand-typed JSON fixture, not on any independently-run review, and the doc presents this as live independent verification.

findings.title: Product gate 'verifies' code by string-matching the test source file and counting test( calls
findings.evidence: src/core.ts:435-438: executionOk/evidenceOk/safetyOk are computed as hasAll(tests, [...string literals...]) where `tests = readIfExists(src/core.test.ts)` (core.ts:427). e.g. safetyOk (line 437) just checks the test FILE contains the strings 'unsafe-host auth does not leak tokens', 'readonly shell allowlist rejects mutating git output flags', etc. regressionOk (line 438) = countTests(tests) >= 49 (countTests at core.ts:269 counts /\btest\(/g occurrences) plus three more literal strings. acceptanceOk (line 433) = hasPassingMatrixRows(roadmap,...) which parses the markdown table in FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md (core.ts:268) — a doc the author writes.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Claim (HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:39 / roadmap 'Anti-self-deception critic ... result compared against PRD, not against implementation-friendly artifacts'): the gate is anti-rubber-stamp. Reality: the gate's pass conditions ARE implementation-friendly artifacts — markdown tables and the presence of specific strings in a test file the author controls. A passing acceptance matrix just means the author typed 'PASS' in 16 rows and named 16 areas the gate greps for.
findings.recommendation: Replace string-presence checks with checks that execute behavior and assert outcomes. Counting test() and grepping test source proves nothing about whether the asserted behaviors hold.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding's literal claims about core.ts are verified. core.ts:427 sets tests=readIfExists(src/core.test.ts). core.ts:435-438: executionOk/evidenceOk/safetyOk/regressionOk are computed via hasAll(tests,[string literals]). safetyOk (core.ts:437) and evidenceOk (core.ts:436) are PURE string-presence checks on the test SOURCE file — they execute nothing and verify no test actually passes; a developer could rename a passing test to a failing stub keeping the name and the gate would not notice. regressionOk (core.ts:438) gates on countTests(tests)>=49 where countTests (core.ts:269) counts /\btest\(/g — and the author's OWN negative test games this with 'test('.repeat(49) (core.test.ts:982). acceptanceOk (core.ts:433) = hasPassingMatrixRows(roadmap,...) parsing the author-written markdown table (core.ts:268,430), and hardGateRows (core.ts:365-367) plus core.ts:441 likewise grep author-written HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md. So the 'anti-rubber-stamp' gate genuinely contains implementation-friendly, author-controlled pass conditions — the finding's thesis holds.

HOWEVER the finding overstates by omission. The gate is NOT purely string/markdown: it execFileSync's the built CLI (core.ts:428-429), executes scripts/live-integration-smoke.mjs and checks its JSON (core.ts:442), opens real run.yaml/process.json/scheduler.json/manifest.json artifacts and verifies structural coherence INCLUDING a SHA-256 patch-digest match manifest.sha256===patchDigest (core.ts:407-408), scans contradictory run states (core.ts:369-386), and reconciles runs (core.ts:297-307). The author's four negative tests (core.test.ts:940,969,990,1019) prove a repo stuffed with the right strings but lacking coherent artifacts/correct digests FAILS — e.g. 'forged dogfood proposal digest fails' (core.test.ts:1019-1051). So string-matching is necessary-but-not-sufficient; real-artifact and digest checks defeat naive fakes.

The finding also MISSED the strongest self-certification vector: the 'independent review' gate (core.ts:319-364) is cryptographically elaborate yet entirely self-issued. writePassingReviewGate (core.test.ts:21-37) shows the author writes APPROVE/CLEAR markdown about their own work, hashes it, writes the hashes into independent-review-gate.json, and reviewArtifactOk trusts that internally-consistent chain. The 'independent review that attacks the 90/95% claim' reduces to any 25+ word file containing 'code-reviewer'+'Recommendation: APPROVE' lacking words like fake/stub/todo (core.ts:330,342). There is no external attestation. This deepens the finding's spirit while showing it aimed at the weaker checks.

Net: thesis correct (several load-bearing pass conditions ARE author-controlled artifacts/markdown, contradicting the doc's anti-rubber-stamp marketing at HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md), but 'critical' overstates because substantive non-string verification (CLI exec, live-smoke exec, artifact coherence, SHA-256 digest match) genuinely exists and blocks simple fakes.

findings.title: Entire runtime/harness source tree (the touted first-class runtime) is untracked in git — never committed
findings.evidence: `git ls-files src/runtime src/harness src/events src/memory src/projection src/composition src/policy` returns 0 files. HEAD is d3daaeb; recent commits touch only src/cli.ts, src/core.ts, src/core.test.ts and docs. All of src/runtime/* (codex-adapter, codex-app-server-bridge, omx/agy/external-cli/shell adapters), src/harness/* (full-target-gate, runtime-gate, verifier, codex-lifecycle-exercise, m8-boundary-evidence, ui-agreement), src/events/ledger.ts, src/projection/*, src/memory/*, src/composition/*, src/policy/*, AND src/runtime-architecture.test.ts and docs/architecture/ are `git ls-files --others` (untracked).
findings.claim_vs_reality: Claim (FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:245-246, README): real Manager/Worker/Reviewer adapter execution, real executor adapter, Codex lifecycle — implemented. Reality: the code that supposedly implements all of that is not part of the committed repository at all. The committed product is only cli.ts + core.ts + core.test.ts + docs. A reviewer pulling HEAD would not even get a compiling project (core.ts imports from the untracked trees).
findings.recommendation: Commit (or delete) the runtime/harness trees and confirm `npm run build` works from a clean checkout of HEAD. Completion claims about uncommitted code are not auditable and must be retracted.

verdict.reasoning:
Independently verified every claim. `git ls-files src/runtime src/harness src/events src/memory src/projection src/composition src/policy` returns 0 files. `git ls-tree -r HEAD --name-only` for src/ returns exactly three files: src/cli.ts, src/core.test.ts, src/core.ts — nothing else. All 24 runtime/harness/events/memory/projection/composition/policy files plus src/runtime-architecture.test.ts and docs/architecture/PHYSICAL_RUNTIME_ARCHITECTURE.md are untracked (git ls-files --others --exclude-standard).

Decisive proof the committed HEAD does not compile: the TRACKED src/core.ts imports the untracked modules at core.ts:6-13 (./events/ledger.js, ./projection/projection.js, ./projection/sqlite-store.js, ./composition/composition.js, ./runtime/codex-process-bridge.js, ./policy/permission-broker.js, ./runtime/omx-adapter.js, ./runtime/agy-adapter.js). The tracked src/cli.ts imports untracked harness/events modules at cli.ts:7-12 (./events/ledger.js, ./harness/full-target-gate.js, ./harness/m8-boundary-evidence.js, ./harness/codex-lifecycle-exercise.js, ./harness/ui-agreement.js, ./harness/full-target-verifier.js). `git ls-tree -r HEAD` confirms NONE of these imported modules exist in the HEAD tree. A fresh clone at d3daaeb yields dangling imports and tsc fails immediately — the committed product does not build.

Not a .gitignore artifact: .gitignore only excludes .omx/, .agent/, node_modules/, dist/, build/, coverage/, .env* — none of the source trees match, so they are genuinely uncommitted.

Doc claims rest on this uncommitted code: FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:245 (v1 role execution via adapter — PASS), :246 (Executor adapter real process lifecycle — PASS), and harness/Codex-lifecycle rows certify behavior implemented only in untracked trees. The 95-passing-tests claim depends on src/runtime-architecture.test.ts which is itself untracked. Only nuance (does not reduce severity): files exist in the working tree so the author's local machine builds/tests pass; the failure is purely at the git/repository-integrity level, exactly as the finding states.

findings.title: Codex launch/attach/stream/interrupt/resume/fork lifecycle never actually runs — 'supported' only existed as a one-time author-machine artifact
findings.evidence: EMPIRICAL: created a real codex-executor run in a fresh repo and started it. .agent/runs/<new>/codex-launch-proof.json => status 'unproven', transcript None, note 'Codex CLI binary detected; no live interactive transcript attached'. `runtime full-target-gate <new-run>` => DECISION FAIL, 13/16 reqs FAIL (web goal input, codex launch attach stream, approval top lane, interrupt, resume, fork, parallel blackboard, sequential handoff, learning memory, review boundary, commit/push approval boundary, ledger UI agreement). Root cause: createCodexLaunchProof (codex-process-bridge.ts:45-92) reaches status 'supported' ONLY if a transcript is found via discoverCodexTranscriptPath(), which requires process.env.CODEX_THREAD_ID (codex-process-bridge.ts:9, 28-30). CODEX_THREAD_ID is empty in normal operation. The interrupt/resume/fork proofs come from CodexAppServerJsonRpcBridge (codex-app-server-bridge.ts) which is wired only behind `agent runtime codex-lifecycle-proof --thread-id` (cli.ts:68), never in startRun. The CodexCliAdapter's resume/fork/interrupt all return 'unproven' stubs (codex-adapter.ts:78-81).
findings.claim_vs_reality: Claim (HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md 'Real Agent Runtime Gate' PASS; FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP matrix 'v1 role execution / Executor adapter PASS'; README 'Codex app-server resume/fork/interrupt lifecycle'). Reality: real runs produce 'unproven' codex evidence and the full-target-gate fails 13/16. The single passing run is the untracked .agent/runs/run-20260601071847719-... directory whose events.jsonl I confirmed contains hand-seeded runtime.lifecycle.supported resume/fork/interrupt events and whose codex-launch-proof points at /Users/dominic/.codex/sessions/.../rollout-2026-06-01T15-20-58-....jsonl — an author-machine transcript not reproducible by anyone else. That run dir is untracked (never committed).
findings.recommendation: Either implement startRun to actually drive the codex app-server bridge and capture a live transcript, or relabel codex/interrupt/resume/fork as unimplemented. Remove the seeded run dir from the completion narrative; a gate that only passes on a non-reproducible hand-built artifact certifies nothing.

verdict.reasoning:
The core technical claim is empirically confirmed. EMPIRICAL TEST: I created a fresh codex-executor run in a clean repo (/tmp/codex-verify-test) and started it. Result: codex-launch-proof.json status='unproven', transcript=None, note 'Codex CLI binary detected; no live interactive transcript attached, so lifecycle remains unproven.' `runtime full-target-gate` => DECISION FAIL, exactly 13/16 FAIL, matching the finding's list verbatim (web goal input, codex launch attach stream, approval top lane, interrupt, resume, fork, parallel blackboard, sequential handoff, learning memory, review boundary, commit approval boundary, push deploy approval boundary, ledger projection UI render agreement).

ROOT CAUSE confirmed by code: startRun (core.ts:136) for codex executors calls ONLY createCodexLaunchProof — never the lifecycle bridge. createCodexLaunchProof reaches 'supported' only if a transcript is attached (codex-process-bridge.ts:50-55), which requires discoverCodexTranscriptPath() → process.env.CODEX_THREAD_ID + cwd===process.cwd() (codex-process-bridge.ts:9,28-30), empty in normal use. CodexAppServerJsonRpcBridge.proveResume/proveFork/proveInterrupt (codex-app-server-bridge.ts:35-55) is invoked ONLY from exerciseCodexAppServerLifecycle (codex-lifecycle-exercise.ts:41-70), reachable ONLY via `agent runtime codex-lifecycle-proof --thread-id` (cli.ts:68) — grep confirms no other non-test callers. CodexCliAdapter.resume/fork/interrupt/approve all return 'unproven' stubs (codex-adapter.ts:78-81).

THE SINGLE PASSING RUN confirmed: .agent/runs/run-20260601071847719-... has full-target-gate.json=PASS (16/16), codex-launch-proof.json status='supported' pointing at /Users/dominic/.codex/sessions/2026/06/01/rollout-...jsonl (author-machine path), and 17 runtime.lifecycle.supported events labeled runtime_label=codex_app_server (the only producer of these, never wired into startRun). `git ls-files .agent/` returns nothing — entire tree untracked, never committed.

DOC CLAIMS confirmed: DOGFOOD_REPORT.md:44 explicitly claims 'Codex app-server resume/fork/interrupt proof, full-target gate PASS, full-target verifier PASS ... for the target run.' CURRENT_BASELINE_GAP_REPORT.md:14-15,63 echo this. The 95 completion ceiling from runProductGate is doc-string-grep based: acceptanceOk=hasPassingMatrixRows(roadmap, requiredRows) (core.ts:433) just checks the roadmap markdown contains PASS rows; it never executes any codex lifecycle.

WHY PARTIALLY (not fully) CONFIRMED — finding's CLAIM_VS_REALITY mis-attributes: (1) README contains NO 'Codex app-server resume/fork/interrupt lifecycle' claim (grep of README.md for codex/app-server/resume/fork/interrupt/lifecycle returns empty). (2) HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:24 'Real Agent Runtime Gate PASS' is scoped to the command/process adapter ('Start from UI launches the task adapter ... explicit shell is hidden'), not codex. (3) FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:246 'Executor adapter PASS' is justified by 'process adapter uses absolute node path, Homebrew PATH, logs' — again the command adapter; roadmap:259 explicitly carves out 'custom Agents SDK runtime' as out-of-scope. So the codex lifecycle is genuinely never run in real operation and 'supported' only ever existed as a one-time author-machine artifact (core claim TRUE), but the specific doc-claim attributions are partly inaccurate.

findings.title: Default 'task adapter' executor is a no-op shell echo, mislabeled as a real runtime
findings.evidence: src/core.ts:128: defaultCommand = `node -e "console.log('Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed')"`. For executor 'command' this is what 'Start task adapter' runs (core.ts:130, 142 runCommand). The dogfood gate's basic-run validation literally asserts stdout includes 'Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed' (core.ts:404). runtimeTruthForRun labels command runs 'primitive_shell' / 'compatibility shell, not first-class Codex/OMX/agy runtime' (core.ts:180).
findings.claim_vs_reality: Claim (HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md 'Real Agent Runtime Gate': 'Start from UI launches the task adapter by default'; matrix 'v1 role execution ... UI now labels Start as task adapter'). Reality: the 'task adapter' default executes a one-line console.log that does no agent work. Renaming the primitive shell 'task adapter' in the UI does not make role/agent execution real. There is no LLM/agent in the default path — it is a process spawner.
findings.recommendation: Stop calling the console.log default a 'task adapter'. The honest description is 'runs an arbitrary local shell command and captures stdout/exit code.' Manager/Worker/Reviewer 'execution' is the same shell command run 3x with a ROLE env var (core.ts:151-160), not three reasoning agents.

verdict.reasoning: All cited code verified independently. src/core.ts:128 defines defaultCommand = `node -e "console.log('Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed')"` — a one-line echo with no agent/LLM. src/core.ts:130 selects this default only when meta.executor === 'command', which is exactly what the UI Start button posts. src/core.ts:499 confirms the UI button text is literally `<button class="primary">Start task adapter</button>`, with explicit shell hidden under `<details><summary>Advanced shell command</summary>`. src/core.ts:142 runs it via plain runCommand (shell spawn, no agent semantics). The dogfood/parity gate self-certifies this no-op: src/core.ts:404 asserts basicProcess.stdout includes 'Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed' as its PASS condition. The code itself contradicts the 'real runtime' framing: core.ts:132 sets runtime_label 'primitive_shell', evidence_status 'primitive', first_class false; core.ts:144 emits 'command executor uses primitive shell'; core.ts:180 runtimeTruthForRun returns label 'primitive_shell' with evidence 'compatibility shell, not first-class Codex/OMX/agy runtime'. The codex/omx/agy adapters (real launch paths at core.ts:136-137) are NOT on the default Start path. Docs verified: HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:24 marks 'Real Agent Runtime Gate' PASS justified only by 'Start from UI launches the task adapter by default'; FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:245 marks 'v1 role execution — Manager/Worker/Reviewer actually execute via adapter' PASS, citing only that the UI 'labels Start as task adapter rather than generic shell'. Renaming a no-op echo 'task adapter' does not make agent/role execution real. The one mitigating nuance: the engine is internally honest (self-labels primitive_shell, surfaces 'Runtime truth' in UI), so the claim-vs-reality deception lives in the milestone docs and gate naming, not in the runtime telemetry.

findings.title: OMX and agy 'adapters' are 3-line detection-only shells of an external-cli stub
findings.evidence: src/runtime/omx-adapter.ts and agy-adapter.ts are each 3 lines extending ExternalCliAdapter (60-line external-cli-adapter.ts). runtime-gate.ts:79 itself lists current_target_gaps including 'OMX adapter missing', 'agy adapter missing'. The matrix nonetheless reports 'Executor adapter PASS' (roadmap:246).
findings.claim_vs_reality: Claim: OMX/Codex/Agents-SDK executor adapters (PRD vision; matrix Executor adapter PASS). Reality: OMX/agy are detection-only wrappers; the gate code's own current_target_gaps admits they are missing. Only the primitive shell path actually executes.
findings.recommendation: Mark OMX/agy as unimplemented in the matrix, or remove the PASS. The gate's own gap list contradicts the matrix.

verdict.reasoning: Verified by direct code reading. (1) src/runtime/omx-adapter.ts:1-3 and src/runtime/agy-adapter.ts:1-3 are each literally 3 lines, trivial subclasses adding zero behavior over ExternalCliAdapter. (2) src/runtime/external-cli-adapter.ts is exactly 60 lines and is the whole implementation; its launch() (lines 43-52) only runs `command -v <binary>` detection (detectExternalCli, lines 8-19), sniffs OMX_SESSION_ID/TMUX_PANE env vars (hasCurrentOmxRuntime, line 21), writes an evidence JSON, and yields ONE event — it never spawns the external CLI, never streams agent output, and never passes the prompt to any process. attach/stream/resume/fork yield static runtime.lifecycle.unproven events (lines 54-59); approve/interrupt return unsupportedResult (lines 56-57). It is detection + env-sniffing + evidence-file writing only. (3) core.ts:137 wires omx/agy executors to this detection-only adapter, then core.ts:144 emits a shell-adapter event explicitly noting 'primitive shell evidence cannot prove first-class runtime lifecycle'; the only executor that runs a real process is 'command'/shell (core.ts:144, evidence_status 'primitive'). (4) runtime-gate.ts:79 hardcodes current_target_gaps to include 'OMX adapter missing' and 'agy adapter missing' even when decision==='PASS' — the gate self-admits both adapters are missing. (5) FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:246 reports 'Executor adapter | PASS'. Nuance lowering severity from critical: the matrix row's own evidence text scopes the PASS to the generic process/shell adapter ('process adapter uses absolute node path, Homebrew PATH, logs, and explicit-shell confirmation'), not to OMX/agy; and the gate code is internally honest about the gap. But the PRD vision (roadmap:123 'Implement OMX/Codex command templates') promises OMX/Codex/Agents-SDK adapters, and the headline 'Executor adapter PASS' papers over the fact that two of three named first-class runtimes are non-executing detection-only shells — exactly the claim-vs-reality gap the finding describes.

findings.title: Test suite is order/state dependent — 'all tests pass' depends on the build-clean path
findings.evidence: `npm test` (= npm run build && node --test dist/**/*.test.js) => tests 95, pass 95, fail 0, exit 0. But running the compiled files directly without the preceding clean build, `node --test dist/core.test.js dist/runtime-architecture.test.js`, reproducibly reported tests 95, pass 94, fail 1. The tests mutate the working-tree .agent/ store, so results depend on prior state/ordering.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Claim (VERIFIED FACTS / DOGFOOD): 95 tests, 95 pass. Reality: true only via the exact npm test path; the suite is not isolated and can show a failure when run against an existing .agent/ state. This is a robustness/credibility concern for a project whose entire completion story rests on test presence.
findings.recommendation: Isolate each test in a temp dir (mkdtemp) instead of mutating the repo's working-tree .agent/. Order-dependent green is fragile evidence.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is reproducible and the central claim holds. Evidence:

1. Canonical path is NOT reliably green: `npm test` (package.json:11 = `npm run build && node --test dist/**/*.test.js`) produced tests 95 / pass 94 / fail 1 on my run — the failure was `unsafe-host auth does not leak tokens on unmatched POST` with ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:15189 (core.test.ts:782). So the "VERIFIED FACTS: 95 pass" is not a stable property even via npm test.

2. Direct-node path is reproducibly flaky: `node --test dist/core.test.js dist/runtime-architecture.test.js` over 5 consecutive runs (same compiled artifacts, same cwd) gave 95/0, 95/0, 95/0, 95/0, 93/2. The 2 failures were `hard completion ceiling requires independent review and reconciliation artifacts` (core.test.ts:924) and `product gate durable report contains report_path after hard gates pass` (core.test.ts:956), which both assert decision === 'PASS'.

3. cwd/state dependence is decisive: running the same compiled test from /tmp reliably fails exactly those two product-gate tests, because they invoke runProductGate(process.cwd()) against the repo's working-tree .agent/ store.

4. No isolation: there are NO beforeEach/afterEach teardown hooks. Tests at core.test.ts:561-592, 924-937, 956-966 read and mutate shared working-tree state — line 926 rmSync deletes .agent/independent-review-gate.json, lines 572/589/930/958 rewrite it, lines 564-567 write forged review artifacts into .agent/review-gates/. runProductGate (core.ts:417-448) depends on live reconcileRuns over .agent/runs/ (core.ts:419,445), the mutable review gate JSON (core.ts:446-447), and a live-smoke SUBPROCESS run on a random port 18000+random(2000) (core.ts:442; scripts/live-integration-smoke.mjs:17) whose status feeds liveSmokeOk -> hardGatesAllPass.

Nuance: the finding pins the cause on .agent/ state mutation/ordering. That is real and proven (the /tmp test is unambiguous). But the dominant transient in my reproduction is the live-smoke/network subprocess flakiness flipping liveSmokeOk, a related-but-distinct robustness defect, and the finding does not name the precise failing tests. Hence the underlying defect (non-isolated, state/network-dependent, self-certifying gate) is fully confirmed even though the exact mechanism is slightly broader than stated. Original .agent/ store was backed up and restored after testing.

findings.title: What genuinely works: a modest local control plane (worth crediting accurately)
findings.evidence: Verified real behavior: CLI project/task/run CRUD and SSR web UI (cli.ts:42-108, core.ts:486-502) with CSRF + loopback-only bind + optional auth token (cli.ts:85-91). Primitive shell execution with detached process tree, timeout/cancel, redaction of sk-/gh*/github_pat tokens (core.ts:150,167) and a readonly-command allowlist (core.ts:169). Real git worktree isolation for multi mode (createWorkerWorktree core.ts:125), actual worktree-diff vs declared-files conflict detection (core.ts:182-186), and an approval-gated git apply --check/apply path with per-patch sha256 manifest verification and overlap rejection (core.ts:240-257). The live-integration-smoke.mjs (54 lines) genuinely boots the web server and drives create-run/start/collect over HTTP — that part is real.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Claim: PRD-scoped local v0-v2 completion candidate at 95%. Reality: the real, working surface is a competent v0 / partial-v1 local prototype. The approval-gated apply and worktree conflict logic are the strongest genuinely-working pieces. Calling it 95% v0-v2 overstates by conflating this real prototype with the uncommitted/seeded runtime claims.
findings.recommendation: Re-baseline honestly: report the working control plane as v0-complete-ish with partial v2 (worktrees/apply), and explicitly mark first-class runtime (codex lifecycle, role agents, OMX/agy) as not implemented. That is a credible, defensible claim; '95% v0-v2' is not.

verdict.reasoning: low severity, not independently verified

dimensions.summary: The deterministic control plane half is genuinely built and reasonably coherent (file-backed registry, append-only hash-chained event ledger, projection, approval-gated apply with digest verification, secret/path guards). But the defining principle "LLM proposes, server decides" is only half-realized: the server decides, yet no LLM ever proposes — there is zero LLM invocation anywhere in the realized code paths, and the runtime adapters are detection/evidence-recording shims, not session controllers. The architecture is also undermined by a 506-line god-object (core.ts = engine + web UI + self-grading gate), a RunStatus state machine with multiple unreachable states and a disjoint projection vocabulary, and the fact that 24 of 27 source files (the entire runtime/harness/events/memory/policy/projection/composition layer the architecture doc diagrams) are git-untracked while the doc and gates self-certify PASS.

findings.title: "LLM proposes" is entirely absent: no LLM is ever invoked in any realized path
findings.evidence: Grep across src for any LLM/inference call (anthropic|openai|claude|completion|turn/start|sendPrompt) returns zero hits outside test files. The codex adapter src/runtime/codex-adapter.ts:8-17 only runs `command -v codex`, `codex --version`, `codex --help`. createCodexLaunchProof (src/runtime/codex-process-bridge.ts:45-92) detects the binary, runs `codex doctor`, and SHA256-hashes a *pre-existing* transcript found via CODEX_THREAD_ID — it never starts a turn. startRun's command executor (src/core.ts:128) literally runs `node -e "console.log('Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed')"`. The only place that drives a real model turn (turn/start in src/harness/codex-lifecycle-exercise.ts:48) is reachable only from the unused CLI subcommand `runtime codex-lifecycle-proof` and is never invoked by createRun/startRun/collectRun.
findings.claim_vs_reality: PRD core principle and README claim 'LLM proposes, server decides' with the LLM as the reasoning layer driving Manager/Worker/Reviewer. Reality: the server decides, but nothing proposes — the 'agent work' is a hardcoded echo command or operator-supplied shell string. The product is a deterministic file/approval state machine with an LLM-shaped hole.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) wire at least one adapter to actually start a model turn and feed its proposal back into the approval/apply pipeline, or (b) stop claiming 'LLM proposes' and re-scope the product honestly as an approval-gated execution control plane. Today the central architectural claim is unbacked by code.

verdict.reasoning: All load-bearing claims independently verified against source. (1) NO LLM inference anywhere in non-test code: grep for anthropic|openai|claude|completion|sendPrompt|chat.completions|messages.create|inference returns zero hits outside *.test.ts; remaining 'prompt' hits are only prompt.md file writes and SHA256 hashing of prompts as evidence, never a model call. (2) The 'command' executor default agent work is a hardcoded echo: src/core.ts:128 defaultCommand = node -e "console.log('Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed')"; non-command executors use operator-supplied shell strings (core.ts:130 options.command || meta.command). (3) Codex adapter only detects binary: src/runtime/codex-adapter.ts:8-17 runs `command -v codex`/`--version`/`--help`; createCodexLaunchProof (codex-process-bridge.ts:45-92) runs `codex doctor` (line 63) and SHA256-hashes a PRE-EXISTING transcript found via CODEX_THREAD_ID (lines 50-53) — never starts a turn. startRun codex branch (core.ts:136) calls exactly this. (4) omx/agy adapters: omx-adapter.ts:2 extends ExternalCliAdapter whose launch (external-cli-adapter.ts:43-52) only runs `command -v`/`--version` and writes evidence; attach/stream/approve/interrupt/resume/fork all yield runtime.lifecycle.unproven or unsupportedResult (lines 54-59). startRun omx/agy branch (core.ts:137) calls this. (5) The sole real turn/start (harness/codex-lifecycle-exercise.ts:48) is reachable ONLY from cli.ts:68 `runtime codex-lifecycle-proof`, a standalone diagnostic; it is NOT called by createRun (core.ts:113), startRun (core.ts:127), or collectRun (core.ts:174), and the web run-start handler (cli.ts:96) routes only to startRun. Minor nuance: an LLM IS technically invoked by that diagnostic (a throwaway 'control turn' to prove interrupt/fork), so the absolutist phrasing 'no LLM is ever invoked in ANY realized path' is very slightly overstated — but it is never invoked in the actual run lifecycle, which is the substance of the finding. Reality: deterministic file/approval state machine with an LLM-shaped hole; nothing proposes. Finding confirmed; CLAIM_VS_REALITY accurate.

findings.title: Runtime adapters are evidence/detection shims, not session controllers
findings.evidence: AgentRuntimeAdapter (src/runtime/types.ts:35-45) declares launch/attach/stream/approve/interrupt/resume/fork, but every concrete impl returns 'unproven'/'unsupported' yields or unsupportedResult(): ShellPrimitiveAdapter (shell-adapter.ts:18-23), CodexCliAdapter.attach/stream/approve/interrupt/resume/fork (codex-adapter.ts:76-81 all return lifecycleEvent 'unproven'), ExternalCliAdapter for omx/agy (external-cli-adapter.ts:54-59 all 'unproven'). omx/agy 'first-class' status is faked from env vars: hasCurrentOmxRuntime checks only `process.env.OMX_SESSION_ID || process.env.TMUX_PANE` (external-cli-adapter.ts:21), so merely running inside tmux flips firstClass=true and emits runtime.session.started with status 'supported' (external-cli-adapter.ts:48,51) without controlling any agent. omx-adapter.ts and agy-adapter.ts are 3 lines each.
findings.claim_vs_reality: README/roadmap claim Executor adapters (OMX/Codex/Agents-SDK) as a real abstraction. Reality: the adapter interface is a contract whose implementations are honest-but-empty 'I cannot do this' stubs; the only non-stub behavior (tmux/env detection) produces a 'supported' session label with no actual session control.
findings.recommendation: Collapse the 7-verb adapter interface to what is actually implementable now (capabilities + detect), and gate the 'supported'/firstClass label on a proven control action, not on the presence of TMUX_PANE. The current env-var path can mint a false first-class runtime claim.

verdict.reasoning:
The core architectural claim holds and is verified line-for-line. AgentRuntimeAdapter declares launch/attach/stream/approve/interrupt/resume/fork (src/runtime/types.ts:35-45). Every concrete adapter's non-launch verbs are honest-but-empty stubs: ShellPrimitiveAdapter attach/resume/fork yield 'runtime.lifecycle.unsupported', stream/interrupt yield 'unproven', approve returns unsupportedResult (shell-adapter.ts:18-23). CodexCliAdapter.attach/stream/resume/fork all call lifecycleEvent -> 'runtime.lifecycle.unproven', approve/interrupt return status 'unproven' (codex-adapter.ts:76-81,84-86). ExternalCliAdapter attach/stream/resume/fork yield 'unproven', approve/interrupt return unsupportedResult (external-cli-adapter.ts:54-59). omx-adapter.ts and agy-adapter.ts are literally 3 lines each (verified by wc -l). The env-var fakery is real and confirmed: hasCurrentOmxRuntime (external-cli-adapter.ts:21) returns true purely from kind==='omx' && cwd===process.cwd() && (OMX_SESSION_ID || TMUX_PANE); so merely running inside tmux flips firstClass=true and launch() emits type 'runtime.session.started' with first_class:true / evidence_status 'supported' (lines 47-51) while controlling no agent. This event can feed runtimeTruthForRun (core.ts:180) which then surfaces '<kind>_runtime' with css 'supported' in the operator UI.

BUT the finding overstates in two ways, hence partial. (1) It claims "the only non-stub behavior is tmux/env detection." False: codex-app-server-bridge.ts contains a genuine JSON-RPC stdio transport that spawns `codex app-server` (lines 62-119) and issues real thread/resume, thread/fork, turn/interrupt, turn/start, thread/read calls with response verification (lines 35-55, hasTurnStatus, forkedFromId linkage check). That is real session-control code. However it sits OUTSIDE the AgentRuntimeAdapter interface the finding scopes itself to, is invoked only by the harness (codex-lifecycle-exercise.ts:31-70) and an explicit cli.ts harness command — NOT by the normal run lifecycle. The production codex run path (core.ts:136) uses createCodexLaunchProof, which only attaches a pre-existing transcript file and hashes it (codex-process-bridge.ts:50-55), not real launch/control. So the finding's adapter-scoped claim survives but its absolutism does not. (2) The omx 'fakes first-class' claim is partly policed: parity-gate.ts:9-15 enforces a 'First-Class Honesty Gate' requiring non-shell adapters to be non-firstClass unless ALL lifecycle verbs are 'supported'; because omx approve/interrupt/resume/fork stay 'unproven', the env-flipped firstClass=true would FAIL that gate, so the fake label is detectable/enforced at the gate layer (though the raw launch event and UI projection still emit 'supported').

findings.title: core.ts is a 506-line god-object mixing engine, web UI, and self-grading gate
findings.evidence: src/core.ts holds: domain types + state transitions (lines 15-27, 114-176), git/process spawning (127-161), redaction/policy helpers (167-172), approvals/apply (191-257), the entire product self-grading gate runProductGate (417-484), AND the full server-rendered HTML/CSS UI renderHtml/renderRun/page (486-503). The web UI string-building (renderHtml line 500 is a single ~3KB template literal) lives in the same module as the apply-patch security logic (applyApprovedProposal line 250) and the gate that grades the project (line 417). No seam separates control logic from presentation from self-evaluation.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Architecture doc diagrams clean layers (Core -> Projection/Policy/Composition/Memory/Adapters). Reality: those are thin satellite modules around one monolith that also contains the UI and the judge that certifies the monolith.
findings.recommendation: Extract three modules at minimum: render/* (UI), gate/* (product gate), and keep core as engine. The gate living inside the graded artifact is also a conflict-of-interest seam (the code that PASS/FAILs the product is shipped and self-tested by the same author module).

verdict.reasoning: Every cited element verified by reading the full file. core.ts is 506 lines (src/core.ts:1-507). It mixes: (1) domain types + state transitions (src/core.ts:15-28 types; createRun 113, startRun 127, collectRun 174, normalizeRunMeta 282); (2) git/process spawning (runCommand spawn shell:true at 150, createWorkerWorktree 125, many execFileSync('git') calls); (3) redaction/policy helpers (redact 167, isReadonlyShellCommand 169, requiresShellApproval 168); (4) approvals/apply-patch security logic (proposeApply 205, applyApprovedProposal 240 with bundle digest validation + git apply --check at 250-254); (5) the full product self-grading gate runProductGate (417-484) which reads the project's own milestone docs (422-425) and hashes src/core.ts itself as gate input (currentReviewInputHash 311-317); and (6) the entire server-rendered HTML/CSS UI — renderHtml (486-501) ends in a single massive template literal (line 500), renderRun (502), and page (503) is a single ~3KB HTML+CSS template literal. No module seam separates these: the UI (renderRun:502) directly calls redact + readFileSync, and the self-grading gate and apply security live in the same file as the UI, sharing private helpers (esc/attr/redact/safeJoin/readYaml). CLAIM_VS_REALITY confirmed: docs/architecture/PHYSICAL_RUNTIME_ARCHITECTURE.md:18-42 diagrams clean layers (Core -> Projection/Policy/Composition/Memory/RuntimeAdapters), but those satellites total only 334 lines combined (policy/permission-broker.ts is 11 lines) vs core.ts at 506; the doc's own sequence diagram even routes renderHtml through Core (line 60), confirming the UI lives in the monolith. The summary's facts (506 lines, redact regex at 167, isReadonlyShellCommand at 169, safeJoin 43, isSecretPath 57) all check out. Calibration: this is accurate but is maintainability/separation-of-concerns debt, not a functional/security defect — the code works and is tested (95 tests). The strongest sub-point is structural: the module contains the judge that certifies itself.

findings.title: RunStatus state machine has unreachable states and a disjoint projection vocabulary
findings.evidence: RunStatus declares 13 states (src/core.ts:17) but grep of all `.status =` assignments shows planning, reviewing, applying, timed_out are NEVER assigned anywhere in the lifecycle — they appear only in the type, in activeStatus() guard lists (core.ts:309), and in the UI active-status filter (core.ts:493). Meanwhile the projection invents its own non-overlapping status words: 'observed', 'running_or_started', 'launch_requested' (src/projection/projection.ts:13,27,29) that never map back to RunStatus. So there are effectively two uncoordinated state machines: run.yaml status and projection status, plus phantom states the UI filters for but the engine can never produce.
findings.claim_vs_reality: A 'coherent state machine for RunStatus/TaskStatus' is implied by the v0/v1/v2 lifecycle narrative. Reality: the lifecycle skips most declared states (basic mode goes created -> collecting -> completed/failed; planning/reviewing/applying are dead), and the projection's status is a third, ad-hoc vocabulary.
findings.recommendation: Define one explicit transition table, delete or actually use the dead states, and make the projection derive RunStatus rather than coin parallel terms. As-is, any operator or test reasoning about 'reviewing'/'applying' is reasoning about states that cannot occur.

verdict.reasoning:
The central technical claims are independently verified and hold. RunStatus (core.ts:17) declares states that are never written by the lifecycle: planning, reviewing, applying, and timed_out are confirmed dead — they appear ONLY in the type (core.ts:17), the runtimeTruthForRun stale read-list (core.ts:180), the activeStatus() guard read-list (core.ts:309), the UI activeStatuses filter (core.ts:493), and the renderRun stale-notice read-list (core.ts:502). A whole-tree grep across src/ (incl. tests) found ZERO write assignments for these four. The other matches are coincidental: timed_out at core.ts:148/150 is the unrelated ProcessLog.timed_out boolean field; applying at core.ts:236 is the "# Apply Proposal" doc string and at core.ts:437 is the "applyApprovedProposal..." test name. So the UI/guards filter for states the engine can never emit — a genuine phantom-state defect.

The projection-vocabulary claim is also confirmed: projection.ts assigns its own status words observed (line 13), started (line 21/23), running_or_started (line 27), launch_requested (line 29) that never map back to RunStatus. The three the finding names as "non-overlapping" are accurate at the cited lines.

The basic-mode lifecycle claim holds: created (core.ts:115) -> collecting (core.ts:127 else-branch / 174) -> completed|failed (core.ts:174 ternary). roles adds dispatching, multi adds workers_running (core.ts:127). The finding correctly does NOT list dispatching/workers_running as dead, since those ARE written.

Why partially_confirmed (not fully): (1) Factual error — RunStatus declares 12 states, not 13 (verified by deduped grep of core.ts:17). (2) The "two uncoordinated state machines" framing is somewhat overstated — run.yaml status and runtime-projection status are deliberately separate views of different artifacts (control-plane metadata vs event-ledger projection), and they DO share completed/failed; they are uncoordinated but not a single broken machine. (3) Functionally the dead states are harmless: guard/filter lists simply never match them, so there is no behavioral bug — this is a coherence/maintainability and correctness-of-modeling issue, not a runtime defect. That makes medium slightly high; adjusted to low.

findings.title: The entire diagrammed architecture layer is git-untracked while docs/gates self-certify PASS
findings.evidence: git ls-files src/ returns only 3 files: cli.ts, core.test.ts, core.ts. All 24 files of src/runtime, src/harness, src/events, src/memory, src/policy, src/projection, src/composition AND src/runtime-architecture.test.ts AND docs/architecture/PHYSICAL_RUNTIME_ARCHITECTURE.md are `git ls-files --others` (never committed). Yet that doc (PHYSICAL_RUNTIME_ARCHITECTURE.md:4) states 'full-target verifier가 PASS' and diagrams all the untracked modules as the live architecture, and the committed HEAD (d3daaeb) cannot even compile against this layer because the imports (core.ts:6-13 import ./events/ledger, ./projection, ./composition, ./policy, ./runtime/*) resolve only to untracked files.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Docs present a committed, reviewed, PASS architecture. Reality: the architecture exists only in the working tree; the repository's actual committed state imports modules that are not in the repository. A fresh clone of HEAD would not build.
findings.recommendation: Commit the runtime/harness/events/memory/policy/projection/composition trees (or revert core.ts's imports). Until then every 'PASS'/'reviewed' claim in the architecture and milestone docs describes uncommitted local state, not the project.

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED facts: `git ls-files src/` returns exactly 3 tracked files (src/cli.ts, src/core.test.ts, src/core.ts). All 24 architecture-layer files under src/runtime, src/harness, src/events, src/memory, src/policy, src/projection, src/composition plus src/runtime-architecture.test.ts are untracked (git ls-files --others), as is docs/architecture/PHYSICAL_RUNTIME_ARCHITECTURE.md. That doc self-certifies "full-target verifier가 PASS" (PHYSICAL_RUNTIME_ARCHITECTURE.md:4) and diagrams every untracked module as the live architecture (lines 23-33, 115, 130-143). The architecture and 33 of the 95 tests live only in the working tree: I cloned HEAD into /tmp, and `git ls-files src/` in the clone showed only the 3 files; `npm test` in the clone ran 62 tests (all pass) vs 95 in the working tree.

REFUTED claim: the finding's central build-failure mechanism is empirically false. It claims "the committed HEAD (d3daaeb) cannot even compile against this layer because the imports (core.ts:6-13 import ./events/ledger, ./projection, ./composition, ./policy, ./runtime/*)" and "A fresh clone of HEAD would not build." But `git show HEAD:src/core.ts` lines 1-5 contain ONLY node-builtin imports and zero local imports of the untracked layer. The core.ts:6-13 imports of ledger/projection/composition/policy/runtime exist ONLY in the uncommitted working-tree copy of core.ts. HEAD's tracked files import only './core.js' (cli.ts:6, core.test.ts:8). I built the fresh clone: `npx tsc -p tsconfig.json` returned TSC_EXIT=0 (clean) and `npm test` passed 62/62. A fresh clone of HEAD DOES build and pass — the committed 3-file subset is internally self-consistent; it just omits the entire architecture layer.

NET: The architectural-integrity problem (a reviewed, PASS-certified, fully-diagrammed architecture plus a third of the test suite existing only as uncommitted working-tree files, with the committed repo being a 3-file subset) is real and significant. But the finding's headline mechanism ("HEAD imports untracked modules so a fresh clone won't build") is factually wrong. Severity is reduced because the dramatic "won't compile" framing does not hold; the genuine issue is that the committed history does not contain the claimed architecture, not that it is broken.

findings.title: Permission broker and memory fabric are near-orphaned seams; full-target gate is self-referential
findings.evidence: evaluatePermission (src/policy/permission-broker.ts) is 11 lines and is called from exactly one engine site (core.ts:134) which only distinguishes 'destructive' vs 'general_tool' — the broker's rich PermissionActionKind taxonomy (12 kinds) and scope logic are exercised only by tests. appendMemoryFact / the memory fabric (src/memory/fabric.ts) is never called from core.ts or cli.ts at all — only from src/harness/m8-boundary-evidence.ts and tests, so the 'learning memory / blackboard / sequential handoff' layers exist only to be filled by an evidence-fabrication harness so the full-target gate can find them (full-target-gate.ts:118-121 require exactly those facts that m8-boundary-evidence.ts:62-66 writes). runRuntimeParityGate (parity-gate.ts) has zero production callers. The full-target gate's 'approval top lane'/'commit'/'push' requirements (full-target-gate.ts:114,123,124) are satisfied by approvals the m8 harness itself creates and resolves (m8-boundary-evidence.ts:48-61).
findings.claim_vs_reality: Memory fabric, permission broker taxonomy, and parity gate are presented as architectural subsystems. Reality: they are largely dead or test-only seams, and the full-target gate is partly a closed loop where one harness writes the exact artifacts another harness checks for, rather than observing organic run output.
findings.recommendation: Either integrate the memory fabric and full permission taxonomy into the real run lifecycle (so facts/permissions arise from actual work) or remove them. A gate that passes because a sibling harness pre-seeded its required artifacts is measuring artifact-shape compliance, not runtime capability.

verdict.reasoning:
Every sub-claim verified independently against source.

PERMISSION BROKER TAXONOMY DEAD IN PRODUCTION: permission-broker.ts:1 defines 12 PermissionActionKind values. The only production caller is core.ts:134, which passes ONLY 'destructive' or 'general_tool' (grep confirms this is the sole production action argument). The other 10 kinds (sandbox_local, specific_unusual_tool, module_composition, upper_scope_memory, system_config, costly, external_deploy, secret_sensitive, git_commit, git_push) appear ONLY in the type union (broker:1) and the broker body (broker:8) — none in any caller except one test (upper_scope_memory at runtime-architecture.test.ts:94). So the broker's scope/risk taxonomy logic (broker:7-9) is exercised only by tests. The 'destructive' and 'general_tool' branch (broker:6) is the only production-reachable code. Nuance the finding states correctly: the broker function itself IS called in prod, so it is not fully dead — but its rich taxonomy is, exactly as worded.

MEMORY FABRIC ORPHANED: appendMemoryFact/writeMemoryFabric/recommendModules are never imported by core.ts or cli.ts. The only non-test caller of appendMemoryFact is the m8 harness (m8-boundary-evidence.ts:63-65). recommendModules has zero non-test callers. readMemoryFabric's only non-test caller is the full-target gate (full-target-gate.ts:107). So the memory layer exists only to be written by the m8 harness and read by the gate.

CLOSED LOOP CONFIRMED: full-target-gate.ts:118 (parallel blackboard) <- m8:63 writes blackboard fact; full-target-gate.ts:119 (sequential handoff) <- m8:64 writes it; full-target-gate.ts:121 (learning memory) <- m8:65 writes module_learning fact. full-target-gate.ts:114/123/124 (approval top lane/commit/push_deploy boundaries) <- the m8 harness itself CREATES each approval via createApproval (m8:86) and RESOLVES it to 'approved' via resolveApproval (m8:94), then writes the approval artifacts into the run dir the gate reads (m8:48-61, 85-95). So one harness writes the exact artifacts another harness checks. Accuracy nuance the finding preserves with 'partly': other gate requirements (codex session at :113, interrupt/resume/fork supported at :115-117, UI smoke at :125) are NOT fabricated by m8 and remain organic/hard, so it is a partial, not total, self-certification.

PARITY GATE ORPHANED: runRuntimeParityGate (parity-gate.ts:5) has zero production callers; only runtime-architecture.test.ts:359-360 calls it. Not wired into runtime-gate or full-target-gate.

GIT STATUS: git ls-files returns nothing for src/policy, src/memory, src/harness, src/runtime-architecture.test.ts; status shows them all '??' (untracked) — these subsystems were never committed, consistent with scaffolding never integrated into the shipped engine.

dimensions.summary: core.ts is a functionally dense but write-only engine: 506 lines holding ~103 functions, 92 of them crammed onto a single physical line, with the longest line at 3684 characters. The logic is mostly correct and the security/lifecycle reasoning is genuinely careful, but readability is near-zero: business logic, git shell-outs, file I/O, run-state machine, the product gate, and the entire SSR HTML template all live in one undivided module with no separation of concerns. There is no real linter (`lint` is aliased to `tsc`), no formatter, and 13 `as unknown as` casts plus a literal-narrowing cast that silently drops task priority data. This is maintainable by exactly one person for exactly as long as they remember it; it is not a maintainable shared engine.

findings.title: One-liner blob: 92 of 103 functions are single physical lines; longest line is 3684 chars
findings.evidence: src/core.ts is 506 lines but defines ~103 functions; `grep` shows 92 functions with their entire body on one line. Line 182 (`updateConflictAndSynthesis`) is 3684 chars; line 503 (`page`) 3373; line 188 (`writeReview`) 3323; line 502 (`renderRun`) 2861; line 174 (`collectRun`) 2815; line 150 (`runCommand`) 2050. These are not one-liners semantically — `collectRun` (174) is a full state machine (status transitions, evidence checks, review writing, approval creation, task status update, event append) compressed onto one line.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Project framing calls core.ts 'the entire engine'; CLAUDE.md preaches readability and evidence. Reality: the engine is deliberately minified into single lines, making diffs, code review, blame, and breakpoint debugging impractical. A one-character logic change shows as a 3000-char line diff.
findings.recommendation: Run a formatter (Prettier/Biome) to physically expand every function body to multiple lines. This is mechanical, behavior-preserving, and instantly recovers reviewability/diffability. Then split core.ts into modules by concern (registry, task, run-lifecycle, approvals/apply, product-gate, html-view). Add Biome or ESLint as a real `lint` script.

verdict.reasoning: Independently verified against src/core.ts. File is 506 lines (wc -l) with 103 function definitions (grep -cE '\bfunction\b' = 103). Every cited long line matches exactly: line 182 updateConflictAndSynthesis = 3684 chars (the max); line 503 page = 3373; line 188 writeReview = 3323; line 502 renderRun = 2861; line 174 collectRun = 2815; line 150 runCommand = 2050. 17 lines exceed 1000 chars, 44 exceed 500. I directly read line 174 (collectRun) and confirmed it is a complete state machine compressed onto ONE physical line: hasStartedEvidence check, status transitions (collecting->completed/failed/blocked), writeReview call, createApproval, updateTaskStatus, appendRuntimeEvent, rebuildIndex — exactly as the finding describes. The CLAIM_VS_REALITY holds: a one-char change in collectRun yields a 2815-char line diff, defeating diff/blame/review/breakpoint debugging, while CLAUDE.md preaches readability and core.ts is marketed as 'the entire engine'. CAVEAT: the precise '92 of 103 single physical lines' count is slightly overstated — my strictest brace-balanced measure reproduces 83/103 (81%), not 92. The qualitative claim (vast majority of functions are one-liners) is decisively true regardless; the 92 vs 83 gap is a minor counting-method discrepancy, not a refutation. Adjusted severity high (not critical) because this is a maintainability/reviewability defect on code that compiles and passes 95/95 tests — it impairs review and debugging, not runtime correctness.

findings.title: Dead code masquerading as a transformation seam: normalizeNoIndexPatch is identity
findings.evidence: src/core.ts:41 `function normalizeNoIndexPatch(patch: string): string { return patch; }`. Its only caller is line 227: `...map((file) => normalizeNoIndexPatch(gitNoIndexPatch(workspace, file)))`. The function returns its argument unchanged, so it does literally nothing.
findings.claim_vs_reality: The name implies patch normalization (e.g. fixing `/dev/null` headers from `git diff --no-index` so the patch applies cleanly). It performs no normalization. This is a no-op that suggests untracked-file patches are handled when they are not — a latent correctness gap in apply-proposal, not just cosmetic dead code.
findings.recommendation: Either implement real normalization (rewrite `--no-index` diff headers to `a//b/` form so `git apply` accepts new-file patches) or delete the function and inline `gitNoIndexPatch`. Add a test that an untracked file in a worker worktree actually round-trips through proposeApply -> applyApprovedProposal.

verdict.reasoning:
The structural half of the finding is TRUE but the correctness/severity half is REFUTED by empirical test.

CONFIRMED: src/core.ts:41 `function normalizeNoIndexPatch(patch: string): string { return patch; }` is an identity function (verified by Read). Its sole caller is src/core.ts:227 inside proposeApply's untracked-file branch: `git(['ls-files', '--others', '--exclude-standard'], workspace).split('\n').filter(Boolean).map((file) => normalizeNoIndexPatch(gitNoIndexPatch(workspace, file)))`. It returns its argument unchanged, so it is genuinely dead/no-op code. The name does imply normalization it does not perform.

REFUTED: the claim that this is "a latent correctness gap in apply-proposal" because untracked-file patches "are not handled." I empirically generated the exact patch the code produces: `git diff --binary --no-index -- /dev/null <file>` (gitNoIndexPatch at core.ts:40). Modern git (2.53.0) emits a clean header: `diff --git a/newfile.txt b/newfile.txt` ... `--- /dev/null` ... `+++ b/newfile.txt`. The `diff --git` line uses `a/`...`b/` paths (NOT the broken `/dev/null` token the finding speculates about), so patchTouchedFiles (core.ts:42, which reads token[3] and strips `b/`) extracts `newfile.txt` correctly, and `git apply --check` + `git apply` (core.ts:253-254) BOTH succeed at a clean repo root, creating the file. The integration test at src/core.test.ts:264-281 ("approved apply proposal applies patch") exercises this exact path: it writes a brand-new untracked file (apply-output.txt) in the isolated worktree, runs proposeApply -> applyApprovedProposal, and asserts the file appears at project root with correct content. I ran that test in isolation: it PASSES (1 pass, 0 fail). So untracked-file patches ARE handled correctly end-to-end; normalizeNoIndexPatch does nothing because git's --no-index output needs no normalization for git apply to consume it.

CONCLUSION: Real but purely cosmetic dead code (a misleadingly-named identity seam). The asserted correctness/latent-gap impact does not exist. Appropriate severity is low (code-quality / dead code / misleading name), not medium.

findings.title: Type cast silently discards data: task priority is force-narrowed to the literal 'normal'
findings.evidence: src/core.ts:108 `priority: (meta.priority || 'normal') as 'normal'` inside `listTasks`. The frontmatter value (which can be `low`/`normal`/`high` per the TaskMeta union at line 22) is read from disk and then cast to the single literal type `'normal'`. The cast tells the compiler it is always `'normal'`, so any non-normal priority on disk is silently typed away (and TaskMeta.priority is declared `'low'|'normal'|'high'`).
findings.claim_vs_reality: TaskMeta declares a real priority union and the init body could set priorities, but the reader pretends every task is normal. This is a genuine data-fidelity bug hidden by a cast — exactly the failure mode `strict: true` is supposed to catch, defeated manually.
findings.recommendation: Replace with a validated parse: `priority: (['low','normal','high'].includes(meta.priority) ? meta.priority : 'normal') as TaskMeta['priority']`. Audit the other 13 `as unknown as` casts (most are `readYaml(...) as unknown as RunMeta`, repeated 8x) — readYaml returns `Record<string,string|number>`, so RunMeta numeric/boolean fields are unvalidated; add one typed `readRunMeta(path)` helper that coerces and validates.

verdict.reasoning:
The cast exists exactly as cited. src/core.ts:108 reads `priority: (meta.priority || 'normal') as 'normal'` inside listTasks, while src/core.ts:22 declares `TaskMeta.priority: 'low' | 'normal' | 'high'`. The cast to the single literal 'normal' is demonstrably wrong and inconsistent: the sibling field on the SAME line casts correctly as `status: (meta.status || 'ready') as TaskStatus`, so the priority cast should have been `as TaskMeta['priority']`. updateTask (core.ts:111) spreads arbitrary `fields` (incl. priority) into the frontmatter, so non-'normal' priorities can genuinely land on disk. So a real, sloppy cast that defeats strict typing for this field is confirmed.

However the EVIDENCE overstates the impact. It claims the value is "silently discarded" / "silently typed away" / a "data-fidelity bug" implying runtime data loss. That is false. A TypeScript `as` cast is erased at compile time and does not mutate the runtime value. I verified empirically: `(meta.priority || 'normal')` with meta.priority='high' returns 'high' at runtime (node test). So listTasks returns the correct on-disk priority string; nothing is dropped. The actual defect is a compile-time-only type-narrowing footgun: any future consumer reading `.priority` off a listTasks() result would be (mis)told by the type checker it is always 'normal'. But grep shows ZERO consumers of `.priority` anywhere (not in cli.ts, and core.ts only sets/declares it; the ProductIndex/UI never renders it), so the hazard is purely latent with no current manifestation.

Net: a genuine but minor code-quality blemish (wrong/inconsistent cast, defeats strict for one unused field), not the data-fidelity bug the finding describes.

findings.title: HTML rendering fused with business logic and unguarded file reads in the same expression
findings.evidence: renderHtml (src/core.ts:486-501) and renderRun (502) build the full SSR page as inline template strings interleaved with index queries, runtimeTruthForRun(), listApprovals(), and filesystem reads. renderRun line 502 ends with `names.map(n=>...esc(redact(readFileSync(join(runDir,n),'utf8')))...)` — a synchronous readFileSync per artifact inside the HTML string builder with no try/catch, so a file deleted between listing and read throws and turns the whole page into a 500 (cli.ts:105 catch). The 3373-char `page()` CSS template (503) is also embedded in core.ts.
findings.claim_vs_reality: README claims a 'full operator Web UI'. The UI is real and server-rendered, but the view layer has zero separation from the domain engine: presentation, data access, and IO error surfaces are one tangle. Changing a CSS color or escaping rule means editing the core engine file.
findings.recommendation: Extract a view module (render.ts) that takes plain data and returns HTML; keep core.ts as data/logic only. Wrap per-artifact reads in try/catch so a transient missing file degrades to a placeholder instead of 500-ing the page. Move the CSS to a constant or static asset.

verdict.reasoning: Every concrete claim verifies against the source. renderHtml (src/core.ts:486-501) interleaves data access and presentation: loadIndex (487), index filtering (491-496), runtimeTruthForRun() inside runCard (499), and raw HTML template strings throughout. renderRun (src/core.ts:502) is one dense line mixing safeJoin/realpathSync path checks, listFilesRecursive, readYaml, listApprovals, and HTML building. The cited unguarded read is real: line 502 ends with `names.map(n=>...esc(redact(readFileSync(join(runDir,n),'utf8')))...).join('')` and I confirmed this trailing map has NO try/catch (the earlier processSummary map on the same line DOES use try/catch, proving the author knew the pattern and omitted it here). A file deleted between listFilesRecursive listing and this synchronous read throws; cli.ts:104 calls renderRun synchronously inside the try, and cli.ts:105 `catch{ res.statusCode=500; res.end(...) }` converts it to a 500 for the whole page — exactly as claimed. The page() CSS is embedded in core.ts (line 503): the <style> block is 3182 chars, and the full page() template line is 3373 chars (the finding's "3373-char CSS template" conflates the two — minor imprecision, the CSS proper is 3182). Net: the architectural fusion of view+domain+IO and the unguarded-read 500 surface are both genuine and accurately located. Calibrating down from high because the IO failure is a narrow local-only TOCTOU that degrades gracefully to a 500 (server stays up), and the single-file fusion is a deliberate design of a ~506-line engine rather than a functional defect.

findings.title: No real linter or formatter; quality enforcement is the type-checker only
findings.evidence: package.json: `"lint": "npm run build"` (i.e. lint === `tsc`). devDependencies are only @types/node and typescript. No eslint/prettier/biome config files exist (`ls` found none). The one-line-per-function style is therefore unenforced and unenforceable by tooling.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Calling a build step 'lint' implies linting happens; it does not. There is no rule catching the empty catches (src/core.ts:77, 149 x2 swallow errors silently), the `err: any` (8x) catch typing, unused-variable, or style drift.
findings.recommendation: Add Biome (single dep, formatter+linter) or ESLint+Prettier. Make `lint` actually lint, add a `format` script, wire both into the test/CI path. Enable rules for `no-empty` (catch the 3 silent catches), `no-explicit-any`, and `consistent-type-assertions` to flag the `as unknown as` pattern.

verdict.reasoning: All claims independently verified. package.json:12 `"lint": "npm run build"` where build (package.json:10) is `tsc -p tsconfig.json` — so `lint` is literally the type-checker, no linter runs. devDependencies (package.json:14-17) are only @types/node and typescript; `dependencies` is empty. A repo-wide find (excluding node_modules) returned ZERO linter/formatter/editorconfig files (no .eslintrc, eslint.config.*, .prettierrc, biome.json, .editorconfig); node_modules also has none. Empty catches confirmed at exact cited lines: core.ts:77 `catch {}` (swallows JSON.parse error) and core.ts:149 in terminateProcessTree which has TWO `catch {}` (the claimed x2). The `err: any` pattern is confirmed and in fact UNDERCOUNTED by the finding (claimed 8x; grep shows 15+ `catch (err: any)` across core.ts:40,84,255,277,506(x3),184,200, cli.ts:82,105, plus runtime/ and harness/ trees; 25 total `: any` in src). Nuance: tsconfig.json:8 sets strict:true, so the type-checker does provide real type-level enforcement — but it does NOT lint, and noUnusedLocals/noUnusedParameters are not enabled, so even unused-variable detection is off. The finding's central claim (no real linter/formatter; the one-line-per-function style and empty-catch/any-cast patterns are unenforced and unenforceable by tooling) is fully accurate and not inflated.

findings.title: Duplicated magic literals: the 'active status' set is inlined 4 times despite an existing helper
findings.evidence: The array `['planning','dispatching','workers_running','collecting','reviewing','applying']` appears inline at src/core.ts:180 (runtimeTruthForRun), :493 (renderHtml activeStatuses), :502 (renderRun staleActiveNotice) even though a canonical `activeStatus()` helper exists at line 309 and is used at 290/293/379. The Korean natural-language-reply regex `/^(진행해|다시|해봐|좋아|ㅇㅋ|오케이)(\s|$)/` is duplicated at lines 302 and 383. RunStatus is declared once (17) but its members are re-listed by hand in multiple filters.
findings.claim_vs_reality: The code has a single-source helper (activeStatus) yet bypasses it in three places, so a new active status would need 4 manual edits and is easy to get inconsistent — the renderHtml/renderRun copies could silently disagree with the engine's own state machine.
findings.recommendation: Export shared constants: `ACTIVE_RUN_STATUSES`, `TERMINAL_RUN_STATUSES`, and a `NATURAL_LANGUAGE_REPLY_RE`. Replace all inline copies with the helper/constant so the state machine has exactly one definition.

verdict.reasoning: All three core claims verified against src/core.ts. (1) The active-status array literal ['planning','dispatching','workers_running','collecting','reviewing','applying'] is inlined at src/core.ts:180 (runtimeTruthForRun), :493 (renderHtml local `activeStatuses`), and :502 (renderRun staleActiveNotice), while a canonical helper `activeStatus()` exists at :309 and is genuinely used at :293 and :379. So a new active status requires edits at 4 sites (309, 180, 493, 502). Minor imprecisions in the finding: it says "inlined 4 times" but it is 3 inline bypasses + 1 canonical helper definition; and it cites usage at line 290, which actually tests processSummary.invalid, not activeStatus (true usages are 293 and 379, plus the local alias at 495/496). (2) The Korean natural-language regex /^(진행해|다시|해봐|좋아|ㅇㅋ|오케이)(\s|$)/ is duplicated verbatim at :302 (reconcileRuns) and :383 (anomaly detection) with no shared constant — confirmed. (3) RunStatus is declared once as a union at :17 but its members are re-listed by hand in multiple filters (e.g. active subset at 180/309/493/502; terminal set ['completed','failed','cancelled','timed_out'] at :496; waiting set at :494) with nothing derived from the type. The drift risk is real: runtimeTruthForRun and renderRun each carry independent copies that could silently disagree with the engine's activeStatus() state machine. However, there is no current correctness bug (the literals are presently identical), so this is a pure DRY/maintainability defect rather than a functional one.

dimensions.summary: The 95-test suite and the elaborate "anti-self-deception" gates are largely tautological self-certification, not independent verification of product behavior. The headline "95/95 pass" is NOT reproducible: from a pristine .agent/ state `npm test` yields 93/2, and after my read-only probing it settled at 94/1 — the failing tests are precisely the "hard completion ceiling" / "product gate" self-certification tests, because they call runProductGate against the real repo and depend on mutable, gitignored .agent/ artifacts that the suite itself writes. The product gate's "independent review" PASS is a JSON file the test fabricates (writePassingReviewGate) with self-computed SHA256s and a fake notification envelope; no reviewer ever runs. The hardest PRD capability (Codex interrupt/resume/fork "supported") is proven only against a hand-written mock JsonRpcTransport — the real codex binary is installed at /opt/homebrew/bin/codex but no test or smoke ever drives CodexAppServerStdioTransport against it. The "UI render agreement" is in-process html.includes() substring matching, never a browser. The gates do contain genuine negative/forgery tests (digest binding, notification-envelope checks) that raise the forgery bar, but the canonical "PASS 95" path is reached by feeding the gate artifacts the same code emits.

findings.title: "95/95 pass" is not reproducible; canonical npm test is actually 93/2 from clean, 94/1 after any probing
findings.evidence: First clean build+run: `node --test dist/*.test.js` => tests 95 / pass 95. After clearing the gitignored trust artifacts (rm .agent/independent-review-gate.json .agent/live-integration-smoke.json .agent/reconciliation.json .agent/review-gates) and a single `npm test`: tests 95 / pass 93 / fail 2. The two failures are `hard completion ceiling requires independent review and reconciliation artifacts` (core.test.ts:924, assert at dist line 851 = source line ~932 `report.decision === 'PASS'`) and `product gate durable report contains report_path after hard gates pass` (core.test.ts:956). Even after re-seeding via `node scripts/live-integration-smoke.mjs`, npm test = 94/1. AssertionError: actual 'FAIL' !== expected 'PASS'.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Docs/summary claim 'npm test => 95 tests, 95 pass' as a VERIFIED FACT and HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md asserts all 7 gates PASS. Reality: the pass count is an emergent side effect of running the whole self-writing suite once against a mutable, gitignored .agent/ directory; from a pristine state it is 93/2 and the failing tests are the anti-self-deception gates themselves.
findings.recommendation: Make the gate tests hermetic: runProductGate must be invoked against an isolated mkdtemp fixture, never process.cwd()/the real repo. Tests must seed all preconditions inside the fixture so the suite is order- and state-independent. Treat any non-deterministic pass count as a release blocker.

verdict.reasoning:
Independently reproduced. Canonical `npm test` glob is `node --test dist/**/*.test.js` (package.json:11), which runs core.test.js + runtime-architecture.test.js together. Across repeated clean runs (clearing .agent/independent-review-gate.json, live-integration-smoke.json, reconciliation.json, review-gates/) I observed oscillating results: 93/2, 94/1, AND 95/95 in the same session. From a freshly-cleared state, clean-run #1 = 93/2 (the finding's exact claim). The failing tests are exactly the anti-self-deception completion gates: "hard completion ceiling requires independent review and reconciliation artifacts" (core.test.ts:924, dist/core.test.js:851) and "product gate durable report contains report_path after hard gates pass" (core.test.ts:956), with verbatim `AssertionError: 'FAIL' !== 'PASS'`.

ROOT CAUSE (confirmed in source): ~5 tests write directly to the LIVE repo's .agent/ via process.cwd() with no temp-dir isolation. Forgery tests (core.test.ts:561, 579) write an INVALID independent-review-gate.json and assert FAIL; completion tests (924 via writePassingReviewGate, 956) write a VALID gate and assert PASS. All call runProductGate(process.cwd()) (lines 573, 590, 928/931, 959). Node v26 schedules test FILES concurrently, so these tests race on the single shared .agent/independent-review-gate.json; the last writer before a given runProductGate() call wins, flipping PASS/FAIL nondeterministically. .agent/ is gitignored (.gitignore confirmed), so a fresh clone starts without these artifacts; the green check is an emergent property of a stateful self-seeding suite, not a clean deterministic pass. Notably, core.test.js run ALONE = 62/62 clean (single-file sequential), proving the race is exposed only by the multi-file glob — exactly the canonical command.

NUANCE / minor overstatement: the finding frames it as deterministic ("canonical npm test is actually 93/2 from clean, 94/1 after probing"). Reality is nondeterministic/flaky: I saw 93/2 roughly 1-in-4-to-8 clean runs, with 95/95 also occurring from cleared state. So the precise counts are not fixed, but the load-bearing claim — that "95/95 pass" is NOT reproducible and the suite is order/state-dependent and self-seeding against a mutable gitignored .agent/ — is fully and repeatedly demonstrated. The VERIFIED FACT "npm test => 95 tests, 95 pass" and HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md's "all 7 gates PASS" cannot be relied upon as evidence of a clean state.
