findings.title: Product gate's "independent review" PASS is a JSON the test fabricates, not an actual reviewer
findings.evidence: writePassingReviewGate() (core.test.ts:21-37) writes .agent/independent-review-gate.json plus reviewer/architect .md artifacts plus fake subagent-notification envelopes, all with SHA256s the test computes itself, and hardcoded agent_ids '019e0000-...-001/002'. reviewArtifactOk() (core.ts:319-364) then validates that file against currentReviewInputHash() and the same self-written notification envelope. Standalone proof: with the file present `node dist/cli.js quality gate` => PASS/95; `rm .agent/independent-review-gate.json` then re-run => FAIL/60. The file is gitignored (git check-ignore confirms) so it never lives in the repo — it is regenerated by the test run.
findings.claim_vs_reality: README/milestone docs imply a real independent code-reviewer + architect approval gate ('LLM proposes, server decides' with independent review). Reality: the 'independent review' is a fixture the test author wrote; the architect/code-reviewer never executed. The gate verifies that the JSON is internally consistent with itself, which any author can satisfy.
findings.recommendation: The review gate cannot be self-fabricated by test code that also asserts PASS. Either (a) bind the review artifact to a real external process whose output cannot be predicted by the gate code, or (b) be explicit in docs that this is a manual attestation slot, and never count it toward an automated 'completion ceiling 95'.

verdict.reasoning:
Every claim verified against actual code and reproduced live.

1) writePassingReviewGate() at src/core.test.ts:21-37 writes .agent/independent-review-gate.json plus .agent/review-gates/code-reviewer.md and architect.md, plus subagent-notification envelopes — all with SHA256s the test computes itself (createHash('sha256').update(reviewerText)..., test:36) and hardcoded agent_ids 019e0000-...-001/002 (test:27-28). Confirmed verbatim.

2) reviewArtifactOk() at src/core.ts:319-364 validates that file against currentReviewInputHash() (core.ts:311-317) and the self-written notification envelope (core.ts:355-359 checks notification.status.completed text-hash equals the declared artifact_sha256). Every check (lines 344-362) is internal-consistency only: artifact text hash == declared sha == notification completed-text hash, agent_id matches a regex, role strings present. All trivially satisfiable by the file's author — and the test author satisfies them all.

3) Standalone proof reproduces EXACTLY (run live): file present -> `node dist/cli.js quality gate` => decision PASS, completion_ceiling 95, independent_review=true. After `rm .agent/independent-review-gate.json` => decision FAIL, completion_ceiling 60, independent_review=false (gate evidence string core.ts:457/470).

4) Gitignored + never committed: git check-ignore -v => .gitignore:3 (.agent/); git log --all -- the file is empty.

Additional independent evidence strengthening the finding: NO production code writes the file — src/core.ts:446 only READS it (jsonIfExists); the sole writers are src/core.test.ts:36,572,589. There is no spawn/execFile of any code-reviewer/architect/codex-review subagent in production (createCodexLaunchProof at core.ts:136 is unrelated run-execution). input_sha256 is merely a SHA256 of the repo's own seven source/doc files (core.ts:311-317), recomputed identically by the test (currentReviewInputHashForTest, test:13-19), so it proves nothing about an external reviewer.

CLAIM_VS_REALITY holds: the 'independent review' that lifts the completion ceiling 60->95 is a fixture the test author wrote; the architect/code-reviewer never executed. The gate verifies only that the JSON is internally consistent with itself.

findings.title: Hardest PRD capability (Codex interrupt/resume/fork 'supported') is proven only against a mock transport; real binary never exercised
findings.evidence: runtime-architecture.test.ts G010/G011 (lines 500-579) inject a hand-written `JsonRpcTransport` whose request() returns canned `{thread:{id:'thread-target'}}` etc. These drive CodexAppServerJsonRpcBridge.proveResume/Fork/Interrupt to status 'supported', which appends runtime.lifecycle.supported events (codex-app-server-bridge.ts:148-167) — the exact events full-target-gate.ts:115-117 require for interrupt/resume/fork PASS. grep confirms NO test/script instantiates the real CodexAppServerStdioTransport; exerciseCodexAppServerLifecycle's real path (codex-lifecycle-exercise.ts:31 `new CodexAppServerStdioTransport()`) is only reachable via the CLI `runtime codex-lifecycle-proof`, never asserted in tests. The codex binary IS installed (/opt/homebrew/bin/codex).
findings.claim_vs_reality: FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP / full-target gate treat interrupt/resume/fork as PASS-able 'supported' lifecycle. Reality: 'supported' is only ever produced by a stub transport returning the exact shapes the assertions look for. The product's real Codex integration for these verbs is unproven, and m8-boundary-evidence.ts itself documents them as 'unproven' (line 39, 78).
findings.recommendation: Add an opt-in integration test (env-gated, e.g. RUN_CODEX_LIVE=1) that drives CodexAppServerStdioTransport against the installed codex app-server and asserts real resume/fork/interrupt. Until that passes, the full-target gate's interrupt/resume/fork rows must be hard-coded FAIL, not satisfiable by mock-fed events.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding holds on independent verification. The full-target gate's PASS for interrupt/resume/fork (full-target-gate.ts:115-117) only checks for the existence of a `runtime.lifecycle.supported` event whose `payload.verb` matches and `evidence_status === 'supported'`. In EVERY executed test, those events are produced without the real Codex binary:
- runtime-architecture.test.ts:505-513 (G010) and :557-567 (G011) inject a hand-written `JsonRpcTransport` whose `request()` returns canned shapes (`{thread:{id:'thread-target'}}`, `{thread:{id:'thread-forked',forkedFromId:'thread-target'}}`, `{}`, and a `thread/read` showing `status:'interrupted'`) — the exact shapes the bridge's `proveResume/Fork/Interrupt` predicates check (codex-app-server-bridge.ts:38,45,53) before appending the supported event (codex-app-server-bridge.ts:148-167).
- The MAIN full-target PASS test is even weaker: runtime-architecture.test.ts:452 directly hand-appends `runtime.lifecycle.supported` events for all three verbs with no bridge and no transport at all, then asserts the gate PASSes (line 466-468).
- The real `CodexAppServerStdioTransport` (codex-app-server-bridge.ts:62, which actually `spawn`s `codex app-server`) is instantiated in only one place: codex-lifecycle-exercise.ts:31 as the default when no transport is passed. grep confirms it is never constructed by any test or by scripts/live-integration-smoke.mjs. Its only live entry point is the CLI command `runtime codex-lifecycle-proof` (cli.ts:68), which is never invoked by `npm test` (package.json:11 runs only `node --test dist/**/*.test.js`) nor by the smoke script (which only exercises `init` and `web`, smoke script lines 16-18).
- The codex binary IS installed (/opt/homebrew/bin/codex -> @openai/codex), so the real path is runnable but is simply never asserted.

The CLAIM_VS_REALITY is accurate: full-target-gate / FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP treat these as PASS-able 'supported' lifecycle, while 'supported' is only ever produced by stub transports/hand-appended events returning the exact assertion-matching shapes. m8-boundary-evidence.ts:39,54,78 itself documents the verbs as 'unproven' until a real bridge executes them, and emits RECORDED_WITH_UNPROVEN_LIFECYCLE.

Two fairness caveats that pull severity down from critical: (1) The project DOES disclose the limitation rather than fully hiding it — CURRENT_BASELINE_GAP_REPORT.md:63 states 'Codex app-server resume/fork/interrupt is proven through a target harness, not an always-on multi-session daemon,' and m8-boundary-evidence explicitly records unproven status. (2) The gate has genuine negative-path coverage: runtime-architecture.test.ts:528-547 (G010 negative) proves the bridge returns 'unproven' on mismatched thread ids, and lines 472-498 prove the gate FAILs interrupt/resume/fork when the supported events are absent. So the gate is not trivially forgeable in the sense that it does require the supported event — but the event itself is only ever generated against a mock. DOGFOOD_REPORT.md:44 does overclaim by listing 'Codex app-server resume/fork/interrupt proof' as accomplished evidence in the same breath as real PASSes, without the mock-transport caveat in that sentence.

Net: the hardest PRD capability (first-class Codex interrupt/resume/fork control) is genuinely unexercised against the real binary in the test/CI path; 'supported' is a self-fulfilling shape match. The finding is confirmed. Severity adjusted from critical to high because the limitation is partially disclosed in the gap report and the bridge/gate logic itself is structurally sound and would correctly mark a real failure as unproven — the gap is test-coverage/exercise, not a forged or logically broken gate.

findings.title: full-target-verifier merely re-reads the artifact full-target-gate just wrote — circular verification
findings.evidence: writeFullTargetGateArtifact (full-target-gate.ts:98-143) computes requirement statuses then writes full-target-gate.json. verifyFullTargetGateArtifact (full-target-verifier.ts:16-51) reads that same file back and its 'checks' are: run_id_matches, decision_pass (artifact.decision==='PASS'), requirement_names_match, all_requirements_pass (item.status==='PASS'), source_events_present, projection_completed. It re-derives nothing independently; it confirms the file the gate emitted says PASS. The Phase 8 test (runtime-architecture.test.ts:469-471) asserts verified.decision==='PASS' immediately after the gate wrote PASS.
findings.claim_vs_reality: A two-stage gate+verifier implies independent cross-checking. Reality: the verifier is a schema/consistency check on the gate's own output, not a re-evaluation of the underlying evidence. It cannot catch a gate that computed PASS from self-fed events.
findings.recommendation: The verifier must re-evaluate requirements from the raw event ledger + artifacts independently (ideally with a different code path/author), or be honestly labeled a JSON schema validator rather than a verification gate.

verdict.reasoning: The finding holds under direct reading. verifyFullTargetGateArtifact (src/harness/full-target-verifier.ts:16-51) reads the same full-target-gate.json the gate just wrote (line 20-21) and computes its six checks ENTIRELY from fields the gate stamped into that JSON: decision_pass = artifact.decision==='PASS' (line 26, literally re-reads the gate verdict), all_requirements_pass = artifact.requirements.every(status==='PASS') (line 28), run_id_matches/requirement_names_match/source_events_present/projection_completed (lines 25,27,29,30) all reparse gate-written fields. It never reads events.jsonl, never rebuilds the projection, never re-runs any requirement evaluator, never re-checks underlying artifacts. By contrast writeFullTargetGateArtifact (src/harness/full-target-gate.ts:100-126) is where the real derivation happens (readRuntimeEvents, validateRuntimeLedger, rebuildRuntimeProjection, runRuntimeHardGate, readMemoryFabric, hasArtifact). The verifier re-derives none of it. The test (src/runtime-architecture.test.ts:466-470) calls the verifier immediately after the gate wrote PASS and asserts verified.decision==='PASS', and that whole test manufactures every event/artifact by hand (lines 440-465) to fabricate a PASS — exactly the self-fed scenario the verifier cannot catch. One mitigating nuance for severity: the verifier recomputes a sha256 (line 22) and the downstream runtime-gate.ts:67 authoritativeFullGate re-checks that sha matches the on-disk file plus runs fullTargetArtifactPasses (runtime-gate.ts:52). That gives post-write tamper detection — but fullTargetArtifactPasses (runtime-gate.ts:52) ALSO only re-reads the artifact's stamped decision/statuses, so the entire chain still trusts the gate's self-computed JSON and never re-evaluates against the event ledger. The 'two-stage gate+verifier' is thus a schema/consistency/integrity check on the gate's own output, not independent cross-verification — matching CLAIM_VS_REALITY precisely.

findings.title: "UI render agreement" / live UI gates are in-process substring matching, never a real browser
findings.evidence: ui-agreement.ts:13-31 calls renderRun() in-process and asserts html.includes('events.jsonl'), html.includes('runtime.lifecycle.supported'), html.includes('full-target-gate.json'). full-target-gate.ts:125 requires uiSmokePasses() which reads that self-written ui-render-smoke.json. live-integration-smoke.mjs spawns the server and does fetch()+string checks (lines 23,47), no DOM/JS execution. No Playwright/headless browser anywhere despite browse/qa skills being available.
findings.claim_vs_reality: HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md 'UX Readability Gate' PASS and 'ledger projection UI render agreement' requirement imply verified operator UI behavior. Reality: it verifies that server-rendered HTML strings contain expected substrings — it cannot detect broken JS, non-functional forms, layout failure, or that the SSE event stream actually streams.
findings.recommendation: Add a real headless-browser smoke (the repo's own browse/qa skills) that loads the home + run detail pages, submits the task/run/approval forms, and asserts the SSE endpoint streams. Substring checks should not count as a UI gate.

verdict.reasoning: Evidence holds. ui-agreement.ts:3,14 imports and calls renderRun() in-process; lines 16-20 are pure html.includes() substring checks (run_id, /api/runs/.../events link, 'events.jsonl', 'runtime.lifecycle.supported', 'full-target-gate.json'); line 29 writes ui-render-smoke.json. renderRun (core.ts:502) is a server-side string-concat function; the "checked" tokens events.jsonl and full-target-gate.json appear only because renderRun lists every file in the run dir (names.map(...)), so the checks effectively just confirm those artifact files exist on disk. full-target-gate.ts:88-96 uiSmokePasses() reads back that same self-written ui-render-smoke.json and the requirement at line 125 trusts status==='PASS' — a self-certifying loop (same process writes then reads the artifact to grant the gate). live-integration-smoke.mjs spawns the real server and uses fetch()+.text()+.includes() (lines 22-23, 46-47) with regex CSRF extraction (line 24) and raw URL-encoded POSTs (line 26): genuinely out-of-process HTTP, but no DOM construction or JS execution. A real SSE producer exists (cli.ts:103: text/event-stream, res.write on 500ms interval) but grep shows zero EventSource/stream consumers in any test — only the link text is asserted (ui-agreement.ts:17). package.json has zero runtime deps and only @types/node+typescript devDeps; grep across src/scripts/package.json finds no playwright/puppeteer/jsdom/headless/chromium. So HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:28 'UX Readability Gate ... PASS' is backed only by substring presence; it cannot detect broken JS, non-functional forms, layout/CSS failure, or non-streaming SSE. Minor calibration: the finding says 'in-process substring matching' — true for ui-agreement.ts (the gate-feeding path); live-integration-smoke is out-of-process HTTP, but still string-only with no browser, so the core claim stands.

findings.title: Many core tests assert on artifacts the same call just wrote (stub adapter output), conflating 'file exists' with 'behavior works'
findings.evidence: core.test.ts:298 asserts executor.process.json stdout includes 'Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed' — that string is emitted by the built-in stub task adapter, so the test confirms the stub printed its own banner. Many multi/roles tests (e.g. 81-93, 240-251) writeFileSync the worker-output .md and work-order workspace files themselves, then assert collectRun read them back. dogfoodEvidence (core.ts:404) keys 'real execution' PASS on that same adapter banner string. The Phase 8 full-target PASS test (runtime-architecture.test.ts:426-472) manually appends every required event and writes every approval artifact, then asserts the gate sees them.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Tests are presented as end-to-end product validation. Reality: a large share assert that the system can read back artifacts the test (or the system's own stub) just wrote, which validates plumbing/serialization but not that a real executor did meaningful work.
findings.recommendation: Distinguish plumbing tests from behavior tests in naming. For 'real execution' claims, drive an actual executor adapter (or the env-gated real codex path) rather than asserting the stub's banner.

verdict.reasoning:
Core claims verified against source. (1) core.test.ts:298 asserts executor.process.json stdout includes 'Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed', which is the hardcoded default command at core.ts:128: console.log('Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed'). The test confirms the built-in stub echoed its own banner — pure self-echo, no real executor. (2) core.ts:404 (basicOk in reconcileRuns, which feeds dogfoodEvidence and the product gate) keys the basic-run 'real execution' PASS on String(basicProcess?.stdout).includes('Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed') — the same self-emitted banner. (3) runtime-architecture.test.ts:426-472 hand-authors every required event via appendRuntimeEvent (goal.received, composition.resolved, runtime.session.started, run.completed, approval.requested/decided, lifecycle.supported) and writeFileSync's every approval artifact, then asserts writeFullTargetGateArtifact returns PASS (line 467) and emits gate.full_target.passed (468). The gate is validated against the exact events the test injected — it proves the gate's tallying logic, not that a real codex runtime produced them. The default executor command is a console.log one-liner (core.ts:128/130), and no test in either suite drives a real OMX/Codex/Agents-SDK executor doing meaningful work.

ADJUSTMENT: The finding overstates the multi-mode tests. It claims tests merely 'writeFileSync the worker-output .md ... then assert collectRun read them back.' That is true for roles tests (core.test.ts:81-93) and the basic banner assertion. But several multi-mode tests do genuine behavioral validation: core.test.ts:120-138 and 140-153 write real files into actual git worktrees (lines 131-132, 151-152), and updateConflictAndSynthesis (core.ts:182) cross-checks DECLARED files (from the .md via extractFilesChanged) against ACTUAL git diff output (collectChangedFilesFromWorkspace at core.ts:185 runs real 'git diff --name-only HEAD' and 'git status --porcelain'). The test at 140 specifically proves the system detects a conflict the worker .md did NOT declare — that is real declared-vs-actual reconciliation, not reading back what the test wrote. So 'a large share' is an overstatement; the reconciliation/conflict-detection logic is non-trivially exercised. The serialization/plumbing critique holds for the banner-based 'real execution' signal and the hand-fed Phase-8 gate, which is the load-bearing concern.

findings.title: Gates trust self-emitted live-integration-smoke.json whose 'root' points to an uncleaned /tmp dir the gate never re-validates fully
findings.evidence: live-integration-smoke.mjs:48-50 writes .agent/live-integration-smoke.json with root=<mkdtemp> and never cleans it up. dogfoodEvidence (core.ts:410-412) accepts liveOk purely from that JSON's own fields (status/exit_code/decision/natural_language_ignored/ui_permission_boundary) without re-opening the run artifacts under root. The gate flips PASS/FAIL based on presence of this gitignored, locally-regenerated file (proven: deleting it -> FAIL/60).
findings.claim_vs_reality: 'Live Integration Gate PASS' in HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md implies durable, re-verifiable integration evidence. Reality: it is a self-reported PASS JSON pointing at an ephemeral temp dir; the gate trusts the report's claims rather than re-deriving them, and the artifact is never committed.
findings.recommendation: Have the gate re-open and re-validate the actual run artifacts under root (or fail if root is gone), and stop treating a self-written status:'PASS' field as sufficient. Clean up temp dirs and record a content digest the gate re-checks.

verdict.reasoning:
Every substantive claim holds under adversarial empirical testing.

(1) live-integration-smoke.mjs:48-50 writes .agent/live-integration-smoke.json with root=<mkdtempSync> (line 9) and has NO cleanup of the temp dir — grep for rmSync/unlink/rmdir/cleanup returned nothing; 273 leftover /tmp/claude-501/do-live-smoke-* dirs are accumulated on disk, concrete proof of non-cleanup.

(2) dogfoodEvidence (core.ts:410-411) computes liveOk from ONLY the JSON's own fields (status==='PASS', run_id is string, exit_code===0, decision==='pass', natural_language_ignored===true, ui_permission_boundary===true). It never re-opens any run artifact under root. Proven: I wrote a fabricated JSON whose root pointed at a nonexistent path (/tmp/this-path-does-not-exist-xyz) with all-PASS self-fields, hid the smoke script so it could not regenerate, and ran runProductGate — Real Execution Gate = PASS and Dogfood Gate = PASS. So the gate accepts a report pointing at a directory that does not even exist.

(3) The gate flips on the file's presence: with the artifact deleted and regeneration disabled, decision=FAIL and completion_ceiling=60 (core.ts:473: hardGatesAllPass ? 95 : 60), and both Real Execution Gate and Dogfood Gate FAIL. With it present they PASS.

(4) The artifact is gitignored (.gitignore:3 matches .agent/), untracked (git ls-files .agent/ empty), and locally regenerated — never committed. The live artifact on disk pointed at /tmp/claude-501/do-live-smoke-* ephemeral roots.

Two precision notes that do not weaken the finding: the '60' in the finding is the completion_ceiling field, not an OS exit code (the CLI exits 2 on FAIL per cli.ts:79); and a second consumer, liveSmokeOk (core.ts:442), actually RE-RUNS the smoke script live via commandEvidence each time the gate runs (regenerating a fresh throwaway root, observed: root changed from do-live-smoke-2v9wD7 to -EzDaIX across invocations) and then only asserts the script's own status==='PASS'. So even that 'live' path trusts a self-emitted verdict against an ephemeral dir and yields zero durable/committed/re-verifiable evidence. The claim-vs-reality framing in HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md ('Live Integration Gate PASS' implying durable re-verifiable evidence) is accurate as stated by the finding.

findings.title: M8 'approval boundary' PASS is produced by the harness self-calling createApproval+resolveApproval (no operator decision)
findings.evidence: m8-boundary-evidence.ts recordApprovalRequest() calls createApproval() (line 86) and snapshotResolvedApproval() calls resolveApproval(approvalId,'approved') (line 94) for shell_mutation, commit, and push_deploy. These self-approvals feed full-target-gate.ts:114/123/124 'approval top lane' / 'commit approval boundary' / 'push deploy approval boundary' PASS. grep: 5 createApproval/resolveApproval calls, all internal.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Approval gates are the PRD's core 'server decides' safety boundary. Reality: in the full-target PASS path, the same harness requests and approves its own approvals, so the boundary is exercised structurally but never gated by an actual human/operator decision.
findings.recommendation: Approval-boundary requirements should be satisfied by an operator-sourced decision in the test/smoke (or clearly marked as a structural-plumbing check), not by the harness approving itself.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding holds on independent code reading. The M8 harness requests AND approves its own approvals with no operator step: m8-boundary-evidence.ts:86 recordApprovalRequest() -> createApproval(), and m8-boundary-evidence.ts:94 snapshotResolvedApproval() -> resolveApproval(approvalId,'approved'). These are invoked for shell_mutation (lines 48/50), commit (56/58), and push_deploy (59/61). They drive the three full-target gate PASS rows: full-target-gate.ts:114 'approval top lane' (via approvedRuntimeAction over the shell_mutation boundary), :123 'commit approval boundary', :124 'push deploy approval boundary'.

The gate is structurally unable to distinguish a harness self-approval from a real operator decision. resolveApproval (core.ts:194) emits an identical approval.decided event regardless of caller, and the genuine operator surfaces -- CLI `agent approval approve` (cli.ts:73) and web POST /api/approvals/:id/approve (cli.ts:100) -- call the exact same resolveApproval. The ApprovalRecord interface (core.ts:26) has NO actor/operator/decided_by field, so no attribution data exists that the gate could use to require a human. The gate predicates (approvedBoundary/approvedRuntimeAction, full-target-gate.ts:67-86) only verify event ordering, decision==='approved', and persisted artifact status==='approved' -- never a distinct actor. The PASS-driving test (runtime-architecture.test.ts:484-485) calls appendM8BoundaryEvidence then writeFullTargetGateArtifact with zero operator interaction, confirming the boundary is self-serviced.

Refinement to the finding's wording: the 'EVIDENCE: 5 calls all internal' count is imprecise. There are 2 distinct internal harness calls (createApproval :86, resolveApproval :94) each fired 3 times, AND resolveApproval is genuinely wired to real auth+CSRF-gated operator surfaces (cli.ts:73,100,101) plus a real policy-enforced approval request in startRun (core.ts:138). So the function is not exclusively internal -- but the M8/full-target PASS path specifically never reaches an operator; it self-approves. The substantive claim (boundary exercised structurally but never gated by a human decision in the PASS path) is fully correct.

Severity: this is a test-integrity / self-certification gap against the PRD's core 'server decides' safety boundary -- a milestone labeled 'approval boundary PASS' that the harness satisfies by approving itself overstates what was verified. It is not a runtime exploit (real operator paths exist and are auth-gated), so medium is the right level.

dimensions.summary: The web control plane's authn/authz layer is genuinely well-built: real CSRF tokens with timingSafeEqual, HttpOnly+SameSite=Strict cookies, loopback-only binding by default, --unsafe-host requiring an auth token, an Origin allowlist, and HTML escaping (esc/attr) that I empirically could not break out of. safeJoin/isSecretPath path containment and the apply-proposal manifest validation (sha256 binding, no-overlap, no-`..` in manifest paths, whole-bundle git apply --check before apply) are real, layered controls — not theater. However, the redact() function is dangerous security theater: I verified it leaks AWS keys, Slack/Stripe/Google tokens, JWTs, PEM private keys, DB connection strings, and npm tokens — it only catches OpenAI and GitHub tokens, yet it is the sole secret filter applied to process stdout/stderr that gets written to disk AND rendered into the web UI. The shell allowlist is also widely mischaracterized as a safety control when it is purely an approval-routing convenience with zero containment. Net: the framework controls are solid, but the secrets-redaction claim is materially false and the command-execution model is more permissive than the UI copy implies.

findings.title: redact() is security theater — leaks AWS, Slack, Stripe, Google, JWT, PEM private keys, DB URLs, npm tokens
findings.evidence: core.ts:167 `redact()` regex = /(sk-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{8,}|gh[opsu]_[A-Za-z0-9_]{8,}|github_pat_[A-Za-z0-9_]+)/g. I ran it against 12 real-world secret formats: it CAUGHT only OpenAI sk- and GitHub tokens; it LEAKED AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE (AWS key id), AWS secret access keys, xoxb- Slack tokens, sk_live_/rk_live_ Stripe keys, AIzaSy Google API keys, eyJ... JWTs, -----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----, postgres://admin:pw@host URLs, and npm_ tokens. This same redact() is the ONLY secret filter on: process stdout/stderr written to disk (core.ts:150 `${label}.stdout.log`, `.process.json`), executor-command.txt (core.ts:131), evidence-errors.json (core.ts:86), AND the web UI run viewer renders `esc(redact(readFileSync(...)))` for every run file (core.ts:502). A worker process that prints any non-OpenAI/GitHub secret persists it in cleartext and serves it over HTTP.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Code presents redact() as a secrets-protection control applied to all captured output; the README/UI imply outputs are sanitized. Reality: it is a narrow two-vendor allowlist that gives false confidence and passes through the large majority of real credential formats.
findings.recommendation: Replace the narrow regex with a real detector covering AWS (AKIA/ASIA + 40-char secrets), Slack (xox[baprs]-), Stripe (sk_live/rk_live/pk_live), Google (AIza), generic high-entropy tokens, PEM private-key blocks, JWTs, and URLs with embedded credentials. Add a unit test asserting each format is redacted. Document redact() as best-effort, not a guarantee, and prefer never capturing secret-bearing env/files into run artifacts at all.

verdict.reasoning: Verified by reading source and empirically running the exact regex. core.ts:167 defines redact() = /(sk-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{8,}|gh[opsu]_[A-Za-z0-9_]{8,}|github_pat_[A-Za-z0-9_]+)/g — confirmed verbatim. Running it against 12 real-world secret formats: it CAUGHT only OpenAI sk- and GitHub tokens; it LEAKED AWS key id (AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE), AWS secret key, Slack xoxb-, Stripe sk_live_/rk_live_, Google AIzaSy, JWT (eyJ...), PEM '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----', postgres://admin:pw@host URL, and npm_ tokens (10 of 12 leaked). redact() is the ONLY secret filter, applied at every output sink: core.ts:86 (evidence-errors.json error text), core.ts:131 (executor-command.txt), core.ts:150 (process.json command + stdout + stderr, plus the separate .stdout.log/.stderr.log files). Confirmed no other sanitization layer exists anywhere in src/ (grep found none beyond core.ts and the path-based isSecretPath, which is unrelated — it blocks reading .env/.pem files but does nothing for secrets printed to stdout). The web UI run viewer renders esc(redact(readFileSync(...))) for EVERY run file at core.ts:502, so a worker that prints any non-OpenAI/GitHub secret persists it in cleartext to disk AND serves it over HTTP. README.md:66 advertises 'rendered command/output artifacts redact common API tokens' — materially misleading given AWS/PEM/JWT/Slack/Stripe/DB-URL pass through. Test coverage is self-confirming: core.test.ts only ever asserts redaction of sk- (line 781/791) and GitHub tokens (line 892/910) — the exact two formats the regex handles — never the formats it misses. One minor caveat on the finding's evidence list: the AWS *secret access key* (high-entropy, prefixless) is genuinely not regex-matchable by any practical filter, so it is a weak example; but AWS key IDs, Slack, Stripe, Google, JWT, PEM headers, npm, and DB URLs all have distinctive prefixes and are unambiguously leaked, so the core claim stands. Severity high is appropriate: a credential-exfiltration / cleartext-persistence-and-HTTP-exposure path with false-confidence framing, though impact is bounded by the local single-operator deployment model (localhost web service, no auth boundary crossed by a remote attacker by default).

findings.title: Shell allowlist (isReadonlyShellCommand) is an approval-routing gate, not a sandbox — any command executes via spawn(shell:true) once approved
findings.evidence: core.ts:169 isReadonlyShellCommand + core.ts:168 requiresShellApproval only decide whether a shell_mutation approval record is created (core.ts:133-138). After approval, core.ts:150 runCommand does `spawn(command, { cwd, shell: true, detached: true })` with the full operator string — arbitrary code execution by design. I verified the allowlist itself: `git show HEAD:../../etc/passwd` and `ls -la /` are classified ALLOW (readonly, no approval), and `cat ../.ssh/id_rsa` / `cat .env` are correctly BLOCKED only from the no-approval path (they still run if an operator approves). The allowlist provides zero containment of what ultimately executes.
findings.claim_vs_reality: The UI copy (core.ts:500) states 'Allowed without approval: git status/diff/log/show, ls/cat safe paths' and 'Blocked by design: secret paths, path traversal', implying the allowlist blocks dangerous commands. Reality: nothing is blocked — non-allowlisted commands are merely routed to an approval the operator can grant, after which they run with full shell.
findings.recommendation: Reword UI/docs to make clear the allowlist only determines auto-approval vs operator approval and does NOT sandbox execution. If real containment is a goal, drop shell:true (use execFile with argv arrays), run executors under a restricted user/seccomp/landlock profile, and treat the allowlist as advisory.

verdict.reasoning: Verified by reading code and empirically running the classifier. (1) requiresShellApproval (core.ts:168) = !isReadonlyShellCommand; its boolean only decides whether a shell_mutation approval record is created (core.ts:133-138). (2) Once approved, the guard at core.ts:138 is bypassed via hasApprovedShellMutation (core.ts:172, matching type=shell_mutation/status=approved/command_sha256), then execution proceeds at core.ts:142 -> runCommand at core.ts:150: spawn(command, { cwd, shell: true, detached: true }) runs the FULL original operator string with NO post-approval allowlist re-validation. (3) Approval is a one-click operator grant: cli.ts:100 /api/approvals/:id/approve -> resolveApproval -> core.ts:194 just sets status='approved'. (4) Empirically ran the exact isReadonlyShellCommand logic: `git show HEAD:../../etc/passwd` and `ls -la /` classify ALLOW with no approval (the metachar regex /[;&|<>`$(){}[]\n\r]/ excludes ':' and '.', and the arg regex /^[A-Za-z0-9_./:-]+$/ accepts traversal); `cat ../.ssh/id_rsa`, `cat .env`, `rm -rf /`, `curl ... | sh`, `cat /etc/passwd` are merely routed to approval, not blocked from ultimate execution. (5) UI copy at core.ts:500 states 'Blocked by design: secret paths, path traversal' but nothing is blocked from execution; the allowlist only gates the no-approval fast path and provides zero containment of what executes, exactly as claimed. Nuance: the design DOES require explicit human approval (operator checks 'Run this exact shell command' and clicks Approve) and SHA-256 binding prevents TOCTOU command swap, so this is human-in-the-loop confirm rather than silent RCE; the genuinely misleading part is the 'Blocked by design' UI wording. Finding holds in full.

findings.title: Web auth token transmitted in URL query string (?auth=) for GET pages and SSE
findings.evidence: cli.ts:91 providedAuth() reads `url.searchParams.get('auth')` first; requirePageAuth() accepts it and even sets the cookie from it (`if (url.searchParams.get('auth') === authToken) res.setHeader('set-cookie', ...)`). The SSE link is rendered as `/api/runs/${runId}/events` and pages are reached via `?auth=<token>`. Tokens in URLs leak into server/proxy access logs, browser history, and the Referer header on any outbound link. Note the cookie-set comparison uses `===` (not timingSafeEqual), unlike requireRemoteAuth.
findings.claim_vs_reality: --unsafe-host + --auth-token is presented as the secure remote-access path. Reality: the primary way to authenticate a browser session puts the bearer secret in the URL, undermining the token's confidentiality once remote access is enabled.
findings.recommendation: Accept the token only via header or POST body / a login form that sets the HttpOnly cookie, and remove ?auth= from GET/SSE acceptance. Use timingSafeEqual consistently for the cookie-set comparison.

verdict.reasoning: All claims verified against source. cli.ts:91 providedAuth() reads url.searchParams.get('auth') FIRST (before x-agent-auth header and agent_auth cookie). requirePageAuth() (cli.ts:91) calls requireRemoteAuth(provided) then, on match, sets a cookie using a plain `if (url.searchParams.get('auth') === authToken)` comparison — confirming the `===` (not timingSafeEqual) discrepancy versus requireRemoteAuth which does use timingSafeEqual with a length pre-check. SSE link is rendered as `<a href="/api/runs/${attr(runId)}/events">` (core.ts:502) and that GET endpoint (cli.ts:103) gates on requirePageAuth(), so a browser must carry the token via the same query/cookie/header path. The project's own test 'unsafe-host auth does not leak tokens on unmatched POST' (core.test.ts:861) authenticates via fetch('http://127.0.0.1:'+port+'/?auth=secret-token') (line 873) and asserts a set-cookie agent_auth= is returned (line 878-879) — directly corroborating that the primary browser-auth path is the URL query string. That test's 'no leak' guarantee is narrowly scoped: it only checks the token is absent from the HTML body (line 877) and an unmatched POST response (line 872); it does NOT cover the finding's real leak vectors (server/proxy access logs, browser history, Referer header on outbound links) — classic CWE-598 credentials-in-URL. CLAIM_VS_REALITY holds: --unsafe-host + --auth-token is the documented remote path (cli.ts:87) yet requires the bearer secret to transit in a URL for the initial session and for any direct SSE/page link. Severity stays medium, not higher: default bind is loopback-only and URL-token exposure requires explicit --unsafe-host opt-in (already flagged as remote command/control risk); the set cookie is HttpOnly; SameSite=Strict; Path=/ so subsequent navigations use the cookie not the query; and requireRemoteAuth itself uses constant-time comparison for the access gate. The `===` cookie-set branch is a genuine but minor sub-issue since requireRemoteAuth already gated access before it runs.

findings.title: git apply has no symlink/path-traversal guard on patch *content* targets
findings.evidence: applyApprovedProposal (core.ts:250-254) validates manifest patch *filenames* against `..` and `/`, verifies sha256, and rejects overlapping targets via patchTouchedFiles, then runs `git apply --check` and `git apply` (no --unsafe-paths, but also no constraint). patchTouchedFiles (core.ts:42) extracts `diff --git` target names only to detect overlap — it never validates that patch targets stay inside root or that the patch does not create a symlink then write through it. Patches originate from multi-worker worktrees whose changed files come from agent-controlled processes (collectChangedFilesFromWorkspace, core.ts:185). isSecretPath is checked on changed file *names* (core.ts:225-226) but a patch that creates `link -> /etc/cron.d/x` style symlink + content is not specifically blocked.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Apply flow is described as 'approval-gated apply' with strong integrity checks. The digest/manifest/overlap checks are real, but the patch payload is trusted to be a benign in-tree diff; symlink-following and creative path targets in the diff body are not defended.
findings.recommendation: Run `git apply` with explicit safety: validate every target in the diff resolves inside root, reject patches that create symlinks or mode-change to symlink, and consider `git apply --3way` in a scratch index plus a post-apply realpath containment check before committing.

verdict.reasoning:
The structural claim is accurate. applyApprovedProposal (core.ts:250) validates manifest patch *filenames* (rejects `/` and `..`), checks sha256, requires `diff --git` prefix, and rejects overlapping targets via patchTouchedFiles, then runs `git apply --check` + `git apply` with `cwd: root` and NO `--unsafe-paths` and no app-level constraint (core.ts:251-254). patchTouchedFiles (core.ts:42) only parses `diff --git` target names for overlap detection — it never inspects the patch body for symlink modes or validates symlink targets. isSecretPath is applied only to changed-file *names* (core.ts:225-226), not to symlink targets in the diff body. projectRoot is just realpathSync(cwd) (core.ts:36), so the apply hits the operator's live working tree.

I reproduced the attacks empirically against the installed git 2.53.0:
- A patch with `new file mode 120000` IS accepted by both `git apply --check` and `git apply`, creating `link -> /tmp/...target` (an arbitrary external symlink) inside the working tree. The app does nothing to block this; patchTouchedFiles parses it as just `["link"]`. CONFIRMED.
- BUT the finding's headline exploit — "symlink + content" write-through to clobber/leak an external file — is blocked by git itself, not the app: same-apply write-through fails ("affected file is behind a symlink"); a two-stage plant-then-write fails with a type mismatch ("link: wrong kind"); and `..` path traversal in the patch body fails with "invalid path" (exit 128). So a modern git neutralizes the dangerous write-through and the traversal-write variants.

Residual real risk: a standalone planted symlink to a sensitive external path in the operator's live tree, which can harm *external* tooling run over the tree (cp -L, tar, build scripts). The app's own readers self-defend (listFilesRecursive skips symlinks, core.ts:201; renderRun realpath/secret-guards, core.ts:502). Test coverage gap is real: apply-flow tests (core.test.ts:236,358,665,679,1036) all use benign mode-100644 patches; the only symlink test (core.test.ts:698) covers renderRun, not applyApprovedProposal — no test feeds a mode-120000 patch through apply.

Net: the missing guard and the symlink-creation primitive are genuine and undefended/untested, but the claimed external write-through impact is prevented by git, lowering practical severity. The patch payload is indeed trusted beyond what the app's checks cover.

findings.title: POST Origin check is bypassable when no Origin header is sent; Host header never validated
findings.evidence: cli.ts:91 requirePostAuth: `const origin = req.headers.origin; if (origin && !allowedOrigins.has(...)) throw`. The check is skipped entirely when Origin is absent (non-browser clients, some same-origin form posts). CSRF token still mitigates cross-site in browsers, but the Origin control itself is conditional. Separately, cli.ts:89 builds the URL from the configured host var and never inspects req.headers.host, so there is no Host-header allowlist (DNS-rebinding hardening) — mitigated in practice by loopback-only default binding.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Origin checks are listed as a CSRF defense layer; in reality they are advisory (skipped without an Origin header) and the real protection is the CSRF token. No DNS-rebinding/Host defense exists.
findings.recommendation: Keep CSRF as the primary defense (it is correctly implemented). For defense-in-depth add a strict Host-header allowlist (loopback hostnames + configured host) and treat a missing Origin on state-changing POSTs as a failure when an auth token is configured.

verdict.reasoning: low severity, not independently verified

findings.title: Default adapter command bypasses the approval/permission flow entirely
findings.evidence: core.ts:128-129: defaultCommand = `node -e "console.log('Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed')"`; operatorProvided = options.command !== undefined || meta.command !== undefined. When neither is set, operatorProvided=false, so requiresShellApproval returns false (core.ts:168) and the command runs with no approval. This is acceptable because the default string is fixed and benign, but it means the 'compatibility_shell' path executes spawn(shell:true) on every basic run with no permission record beyond a permission.allowed event.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Permission broker is presented as gating runtime starts. Reality: the common-case basic run is auto-allowed (general_tool -> allow at permission-broker.ts:6), which is reasonable but worth stating explicitly since it is the default execution path.
findings.recommendation: Document that the built-in adapter command is trusted-by-construction and ensure meta.command (operator-supplied at create time) is always run through requiresShellApproval — confirm a stored mutating meta.command cannot reach spawn without an approval on a later start.

verdict.reasoning: low severity, not independently verified

dimensions.summary: The v0-v2 narrowing is partly legitimate (SaaS, custom Agents-SDK runtime, broad MCP, and auto-push are genuine PRD non-goals or v3+ postponements), but it is also used to paper over stubbed-out core capabilities that the PRD demands inside v0-v2 itself. What genuinely works is the file-backed v0 loop: project registry, task/run CRUD, baseline/collect git evidence, deterministic review/decision, approval-gated code-diff apply, real git-worktree isolation for multi-worker, and a server-rendered web UI. What is nominal/stubbed: the v1 "Manager/Worker/Reviewer roles" are the SAME shell command re-run with ROLE env vars (no role prompts, no LLM differentiation, no manager planning); OMX/Codex "session control" is detection + transcript-attachment only (launch/interrupt/resume/fork explicitly return "unsupported"/"unproven"); promotion-to-skill/workflow/eval is a single hardcoded memory stub with no cause-classification routing. The self-graded "completion_ceiling: 95" is measured against a target redrawn to exactly fit what was built, and its 7 hard gates test UI truth-labeling, not the PRD's differentiating behaviors. True completion against the PRD as written is roughly 30-35%: a solid v0 record-keeping shell, a scaffolded v1/v2, and almost none of the learning/promotion loop that is the PRD's stated reason for existing.

findings.title: Manager/Worker/Reviewer roles are one shell command run 3x with ROLE env vars, not real role agents (v1 PRD core)
findings.evidence: src/core.ts:151 runRoles() runs `command.replaceAll('{role}','manager')` then '...worker' then '...reviewer', each via runCommand with env {ROLE:'manager'} / {ROLE:'worker'} / {ROLE:'reviewer'}. The SAME operator-supplied command string is executed three times. createRoleArtifacts (core.ts:123) only writes static templates: manager-plan.md says 'Create one bounded worker order' verbatim; worker-001.md says 'Pending execution'. No role prompt loading, no LLM call, no manager-derived work order, no reviewer evaluating worker output. README.md role example literally uses `node -e "console.log(process.env.ROLE)"`.
findings.claim_vs_reality: FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:245 claims 'v1 role execution: Manager/Worker/Reviewer actually execute via adapter — PASS' and roadmap:108 'Role prompt templates are executed, not just written.' Reality: role prompts are NOT executed; a single arbitrary command is invoked three times distinguished only by an env var, and the role artifacts are static boilerplate. PRD sections 4.3-4.5 require Manager to restate goal/write work orders/synthesize, Reviewer to score against rubric separately from Manager — none of this reasoning exists.
findings.recommendation: Either implement genuine role execution (distinct role prompts compiled into per-role adapter invocations, manager output feeding a validated work_order, reviewer consuming worker output) or downgrade the roadmap/HARD_COMPLETION_GATES rows from PASS to PARTIAL and relabel mode=roles as 'env-tagged shell passes' in README so the claim matches behavior.

verdict.reasoning: Finding fully confirmed by direct code reading. src/core.ts:151-160 runRoles() runs the SAME operator-supplied command three times: command.replaceAll('{role}','manager') with env {ROLE:'manager'}, then '...worker' with {ROLE:'worker',WORKER_ID:'worker-001'}, then '...reviewer' with {ROLE:'reviewer'}. Differentiation is only a substring substitution and the ROLE env var. No role prompt loading, no LLM call, no manager-derived work order feeding the worker, no reviewer consuming worker output — each is an independent shell process of the identical command, after which only the numeric exit code is appended to a static markdown file. createRoleArtifacts (core.ts:123) writes pure static boilerplate via writeIfMissing: manager-plan.md hardcodes 'Create one bounded worker order and require deterministic evidence', worker-001.md says 'Pending execution'/'Pending collect'. prompt.md (core.ts:119) is a single generic prompt never passed to the command and identical across roles. README.md:36 role example is literally node -e "console.log(process.env.ROLE || \"role\")". The proof test (core.test.ts:847-857) only runs that echo command and asserts each stdout contains its role name — proving nothing beyond the env var differing. CLAIM_VS_REALITY confirmed: FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:108 'Role prompt templates are executed, not just written' is false (no role prompt templates exist or are executed); roadmap:109-112 'Manager produces plan from context / Reviewer evaluates actual outputs/diff/logs' is false (manager-plan is static; reviewer command never receives worker output); roadmap:245 marks this PASS. PRD 4.3 (line 223-236) requires Manager to restate goal, identify context, write work orders, synthesize; PRD 4.5 (line 252-265) requires Reviewer to be a SEPARATE role with separate prompt/context/output contract; PRD line 709 explicitly states the three must have different prompts/context/output contracts. The implementation gives all three the identical command with zero differentiation — directly violating the PRD's central v1 requirement. Minor calibration nuance: the deterministic writeReview() (core.ts:188) does perform real artifact/exit-code gating, but that is Orchestrator-side gating, not 'reviewer role execution,' and does not rescue the absent role-agent layer that is the entire point of v1.

findings.title: OMX/Codex executor 'session control' is detection + transcript attachment, not lifecycle control
findings.evidence: src/runtime/codex-process-bridge.ts:45 createCodexLaunchProof runs `codex doctor` and, in the best case, attaches an ALREADY-RUNNING Codex transcript file discovered by CODEX_THREAD_ID matching the cwd (line 50, shouldAutoAttachCurrentCodexTranscript). Its own note (line 55) admits 'Approval, interrupt, resume, and fork remain operation-specific unless separately proven.' src/runtime/external-cli-adapter.ts:56-59: approve/interrupt return unsupportedResult; attach/stream/resume/fork only yield 'runtime.lifecycle.unproven'. OmxCliAdapter (omx-adapter.ts:2) is just ExternalCliAdapter with no added control. 'supported' status requires the operator to already be inside an OMX/tmux or Codex session (external-cli-adapter.ts:21, hasCurrentOmxRuntime checks OMX_SESSION_ID/TMUX_PANE).
findings.claim_vs_reality: FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:246 'Executor adapter: real process lifecycle/log/exit handling — PASS' and PRD 4.6 lists OMX/Codex Adapters as the initial executors that 'actually perform the work'. PRD 1.1 vision is `agent web` -> 'OMX/Codex worker 실행'. Reality: the product cannot launch, drive, interrupt, or resume an OMX/Codex agent; it can only spawn an arbitrary shell command (primitive_shell) or passively read a transcript from a session the human started elsewhere.
findings.recommendation: Mark the executor row as PRD-partial and document that first-class OMX/Codex control is unimplemented. If real control is intended for v1, build an actual process bridge (the codex-app-server-bridge filename hints at this) and gate the PASS on a live launch->stream->interrupt exercise, not on transcript discovery.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding holds against the actual code. In the production run path, core.ts:136 calls createCodexLaunchProof for codex, which only runs `codex doctor` and, best case, attaches an ALREADY-RUNNING transcript discovered via CODEX_THREAD_ID==process.cwd() (codex-process-bridge.ts:28-30,50); its own note (line 55) admits "Approval, interrupt, resume, and fork remain operation-specific unless separately proven." For omx/agy, core.ts:137 calls ExternalCliAdapter.launch(): approve/interrupt return unsupportedResult (external-cli-adapter.ts:56-57), and attach/stream/resume/fork only yield runtime.lifecycle.unproven (lines 54-59). "supported" requires hasCurrentOmxRuntime — i.e. the operator is ALREADY inside an OMX/tmux session (external-cli-adapter.ts:21, checks OMX_SESSION_ID/TMUX_PANE). OmxCliAdapter is literally `extends ExternalCliAdapter('omx')` with no added control (omx-adapter.ts:2). CodexCliAdapter.approve/interrupt/resume/fork all return unproven/unsupported (codex-adapter.ts:78-81), and its "supported" status hinges entirely on transcriptSupported (codex-adapter.ts:48,54-56). The only first-class fallthrough writes a primitive_shell event (core.ts:144). So the operator-facing product can launch only an arbitrary shell command (primitive_shell) or passively read a transcript from a human-started session — it cannot launch, drive, interrupt, or resume an OMX/Codex agent.

IMPORTANT CORRECTION to the finding's absolutism: real lifecycle-control code DOES exist. codex-app-server-bridge.ts spawns `codex app-server` (CodexAppServerStdioTransport, line 62-93) and sends JSON-RPC thread/resume, thread/fork, turn/interrupt with response verification (proveResume/proveFork/proveInterrupt, lines 35-55). But it is wired ONLY into a manual harness command `agent runtime codex-lifecycle-proof` (cli.ts:68 -> harness/codex-lifecycle-exercise.ts), NEVER into createRun/startRun, and is tested ONLY with a STUB transport (runtime-architecture.test.ts:505-525). So lifecycle control is unintegrated and unproven against a live codex, not absent.

Claim-vs-reality: FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:246 marks "Executor adapter | real process lifecycle/log/exit handling = PASS", but the PASS rationale text itself describes the shell adapter ("absolute node path, Homebrew PATH, logs, explicit-shell confirmation"), not Codex/OMX agent lifecycle — corroborating the finding. PRD partial mitigation: PRD 9.4 (line 828) explicitly scopes the INITIAL version to a "반자동 handoff protocol" where the user performs the actual work in OMX/Codex, which matches transcript-attach for v0. But PRD 1.1 (line 86 "OMX/Codex worker 실행"), 4.6, and 19.2 ("Codex CLI를 직접 제어하는 adapter") frame these adapters as executors that actually perform/directly control the work — which no operator-driven path delivers.

findings.title: Promotion to skill/workflow/eval/memory — the PRD's central learning loop — is a single hardcoded memory stub
findings.evidence: src/core.ts:195 createPromotionProposal always sets target_type:'memory' and target_path:'.agent/memory/project-facts.md' with reason 'Review suggested durable system patch.' It only fires when review.md contains a non-'None.' 'System Patch Suggestions' section, but writeReview (core.ts:188) only ever writes either 'None.' or the static string 'Create blocker-resolution task before claiming completion.' — there is no cause classification and no routing to skill vs workflow vs eval vs policy vs AGENTS.md. There is no CLI command for promotion ('grep promot src/cli.ts' = empty). src/memory/fabric.ts exists but appendMemoryFact/recommendModules are not invoked from the operator promotion flow.
findings.claim_vs_reality: FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:248 'Promotion proposals: real review-derived proposal lifecycle — PASS'. PRD 4.11/5.5 require failure -> cause classification -> propose which of AGENTS.md/skill/workflow/eval/policy/memory to patch -> approve -> promote, and PRD 1.1/2.2 name this the reason the product exists ('어떤 반복 패턴이 skill/workflow/eval/memory로 승격되어야 하는가'). Reality: one generic always-memory record with no classification, no skill/workflow/eval path, and no apply step.
findings.recommendation: Implement actual promotion classification and at least skill/workflow/eval/memory targets with an apply path, OR explicitly scope promotion down in the PRD-delta and stop marking it PASS. Wire src/memory/fabric.ts in or delete it.

verdict.reasoning: Finding fully verified against source. createPromotionProposal (src/core.ts:195) hardcodes target_type:'memory', target_path:'.agent/memory/project-facts.md', reason:'Review suggested durable system patch.'; grep of `target_type:` shows 'memory' is the ONLY value ever assigned at runtime despite the 6-value union in PromotionRecord (src/core.ts:27). Its trigger checks review.md for a non-'None.' 'System Patch Suggestions' section, but writeReview (src/core.ts:188) only ever writes 'None.' or the single static string 'Create blocker-resolution task before claiming completion.' (grep -c confirms exactly 1 occurrence) — no cause classification, no findings-derived content. No lifecycle/apply: promotions are written status:'proposed' and never mutated; applyApprovedProposal (src/core.ts:244-245) rejects anything whose type != 'apply_proposal', so a promotion can never be approved or applied. No CLI surface: `grep promot src/cli.ts` empty; CLI verb list (src/cli.ts:45-80) has no promotion command. Memory fabric (src/memory/fabric.ts appendMemoryFact/recommendModules) is invoked only from src/harness/m8-boundary-evidence.ts:63-65, src/harness/full-target-gate.ts:107, and tests — never from the operator promotion/collectRun flow; the promotion even points at .agent/memory/project-facts.md, a path fabric.ts does not manage (it uses memory/fabric.json). No promotion-content tests in src/core.test.ts (only matrix row labels). FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:248 claims 'real review-derived proposal lifecycle — PASS', contradicted by PRD §5.5 (lines 448-454) and §1.1/2.2 (lines 7,75,89,129,143,451-452) which require failure->cause classification->route to AGENTS.md/skill/workflow/eval/policy/memory->approve->promote. Reality is one generic always-memory record with no classification, no non-memory path, no approval, no apply.

findings.title: Committed HEAD is broken: every advanced module its own code imports is untracked in git
findings.evidence: Committed src/core.ts imports ./events/ledger.js, ./projection/projection.js, ./projection/sqlite-store.js, ./composition/composition.js, ./policy/permission-broker.js, ./runtime/{codex-process-bridge,omx-adapter,agy-adapter}.js; committed src/cli.ts imports ./harness/{full-target-gate,full-target-verifier,m8-boundary-evidence,codex-lifecycle-exercise,ui-agreement}.js. `git ls-files` confirms ALL of src/runtime, src/harness, src/events, src/memory, src/policy, src/projection, src/composition and src/runtime-architecture.test.ts are UNTRACKED (git ls-files --others). HEAD commit d3daaeb and the prior 5 commits touch only cli.ts/core.ts/core.test.ts + docs. A clean checkout of HEAD would fail tsc with missing-module errors.
findings.claim_vs_reality: All milestone docs (HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:3 'PASS', completion_ceiling 95) and the roadmap PASS matrix imply a coherent shipped product. Reality: the '95% complete' state exists only in an uncommitted working tree; the actual committed repository does not build. Build passes (exit 0) ONLY because untracked files are present locally.
findings.recommendation: Commit the runtime/harness/events/memory/policy/projection/composition trees (or fix the gitignore) before any completion claim. A completion ceiling computed against an uncommittable HEAD is not defensible; add a CI check that a clean clone builds and tests pass.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding's central factual premise is false. It claims the COMMITTED src/core.ts imports ./events/ledger.js, ./projection/*, ./composition/*, ./policy/*, ./runtime/* and the COMMITTED src/cli.ts imports ./harness/*, so a clean checkout of HEAD would fail tsc. I verified directly against git HEAD that this is not the case.

git show HEAD:src/core.ts shows imports ONLY of Node builtins on lines 1-5 (node:child_process, node:fs, node:path, node:crypto, node:os) — zero local advanced-module imports (grep for ./(events|projection|composition|policy|runtime|memory|harness)/ returns nothing). git show HEAD:src/cli.ts imports only node:* builtins plus './core.js' on line 6, and core.js IS tracked. git show HEAD:src/core.test.ts likewise has no advanced-module imports.

The advanced-module imports the finding attributes to 'committed' files exist ONLY as '+' additions in the uncommitted working tree: `git diff HEAD -- src/core.ts` shows all 8 advanced imports (./events/ledger.js, ./projection/projection.js, ./projection/sqlite-store.js, ./composition/composition.js, ./runtime/codex-process-bridge.js, ./policy/permission-broker.js, ./runtime/omx-adapter.js, ./runtime/agy-adapter.js) as added lines; `git diff HEAD -- src/cli.ts` shows the 5 harness imports as added lines. They were never committed.

I directly tested the central claim 'a clean checkout of HEAD would fail tsc': cloned HEAD to /tmp/doverify (src/ contained ONLY cli.ts, core.test.ts, core.ts), ran npm install + npm run build -> exit 0 (clean tsc, no missing-module errors), then npm test -> 62 tests, 62 pass, fail 0, exit 0. The committed HEAD builds and passes its full suite WITHOUT any of the untracked files.

git ls-files --others does confirm the runtime/harness/events/memory/policy/projection/composition trees and runtime-architecture.test.ts are untracked (that sub-fact is true), but the committed HEAD does not depend on them, so HEAD is not broken. The genuine, different issue is that a large body of working-tree code (the advanced modules plus the modified core.ts/cli.ts that wire them in) is uncommitted — a 'work not committed' problem, not a 'committed HEAD fails to build' problem. The finding's stated mechanism and conclusion are refuted.

Note for context (not the finding's claim): the milestone docs are themselves modified-but-uncommitted and their PASS/95% state relies on the untracked harness code, so those doc claims partly rest on uncommitted work — but that does not validate this finding's specific, incorrect assertion about committed imports failing tsc.

findings.title: Self-certifying completion: hard gates and quality gate validate exactly what was built, not the PRD's differentiating capabilities
findings.evidence: HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:23-29 lists 7 'non-negotiable' gates — all concern local-web UI state-truth labeling, operator-intent checkbox behavior, UI readability, and the gate's own ceiling computation. None test real role-agent reasoning, real OMX/Codex control, or real promotion routing. The 'Live Integration Gate' is scripts/live-integration-smoke.mjs printing LIVE_INTEGRATION_SMOKE_PASS. `agent quality gate` (cli.ts:79 -> runProductGate) and full-target-gate/verifier (harness/, untracked) are run and graded by the same codebase they certify. composition.ts:38 buildCompositionPlan writes a JSON naming skills:['ultragoal'], agents:['manager','worker','reviewer'] as bare string labels — it selects/loads/executes nothing.
findings.claim_vs_reality: FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:255 'Anti-self-deception critic — PASS' and HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:40 require 'independent review explicitly attacks the 90/95% claim.' Reality: the gates are authored by, run by, and scoped to the implementation; passing them proves the UI tells the truth about shell runs, not that PRD capabilities exist. The completion_ceiling:95 is graded against a target (roadmap:9 'Scope Guard') redrawn to match the build.
findings.recommendation: Either add gates that exercise the PRD's differentiating behaviors (role reasoning, executor lifecycle, promotion classification) with external/independent verification, or stop publishing a numeric 95 ceiling. Replace it with an honest per-capability matrix (works/partial/stub).

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against source. (1) HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:23-29 — all 7 "non-negotiable" gates concern local-web UI state-truth labeling, operator-intent checkbox behavior, UI lane readability, the live-smoke script, and the gate's own ceiling computation. None require a real LLM role-agent to reason or a real Codex/OMX session to execute.

(2) "No real OMX/Codex control" CONFIRMED: CodexCliAdapter (src/runtime/codex-adapter.ts:65-86) returns status 'unproven'/'unsupported' for launch/attach/approve/interrupt/resume/fork — it detects the binary but never starts an LLM session (line 71 note explicitly says so). The thing the gates actually validate as a passing run is core.ts:128 defaultCommand = node -e "console.log('Dominic Orchestration task adapter executed')" — a hardcoded echo. "roles" mode (core.ts:151-160) runs that same shell command 3x with ROLE env vars; no agent reasoning. Roadmap row v1 (FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:245) admits its evidence is merely "UI now labels Start as task adapter rather than generic shell."

(3) Live Integration Gate CONFIRMED self-asserting: scripts/live-integration-smoke.mjs:48-51 writes .agent/live-integration-smoke.json {status:'PASS',...} and prints LIVE_INTEGRATION_SMOKE_PASS; core.ts:442 then greps for that literal token plus the JSON status the same script wrote. It does run a real web server and checks the natural-language-not-captured-as-command boundary (a genuine, non-trivial check) — so the gate proves the UI tells the truth about shell runs, exactly the finding's point: it proves what was built, not PRD differentiators.

(4) "Run/graded by the same codebase" CONFIRMED: runProductGate (core.ts:417-475) parses the project's own docs — hasPassingMatrixRows over FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md (acceptance matrix rows are self-declared PASS markdown cells, lines 240-249) and hardGateRows over HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md — and reads .agent/*.json artifacts the project itself wrote. completion_ceiling=95 iff those pass (core.ts:473). The target is literally parsed from project-authored markdown.

(5) composition.ts:50-57 CONFIRMED: buildCompositionPlan writes bare string arrays (skills:['ultragoal'], agents:['manager','worker','reviewer']); the only consumers are an event payload (core.ts:116) and a test (runtime-architecture.test.ts:20). Nothing reads composition.json back to select/load/execute any skill or agent.

(6) STRONGEST point — the "independent review explicitly attacks the 90/95% claim" closure requirement (HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:40) is REFUTED as a real control. reviewArtifactOk (core.ts:319-364) validates only the JSON *shape* of .agent/independent-review-gate.json (sha256 matches, agent_id regex, source:'codex-native-subagent' string, role labels, notification cross-refs). The ONLY writers of that artifact are test fixtures (core.test.ts:36, plus forged negatives at 572/589). No production code path generates it. The live .agent/independent-review-gate.json (read: status PASS, hand-authored fixed agent_ids 019e0000-...-0001/0002, completed_at 2026-06-01T00:00:00.000Z) was authored to satisfy the validator, not produced by an actual independent reviewing agent. So the anti-self-deception protection validates structural consistency a human can fabricate, not genuine independent adversarial review.

Net: the gates do real deterministic work (truthful UI status over shell execution + operator-intent boundary), but the 95 completion ceiling is graded by the implementation against a target it parses from its own docs, and the PRD's differentiating capabilities (real LLM role agents, real Codex/OMX runtime control, real composition/promotion routing) are echoed/stubbed/doc-only and untested by the gates. Finding accurately distinguishes "UI tells truth about shell runs" from "PRD capabilities exist."

findings.title: v0-v2 narrowing is partially faithful but silently shrinks in-scope v1/v2 capability requirements
findings.evidence: PRD 3 Non-Goals legitimately defer: SaaS/external sync (item 6, line 172-174), self Agents-SDK runtime to v3+ (item 3, line 161-163), broad MCP (item 7), auto merge/push (item 4). The roadmap (FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:9) correctly cites these. HOWEVER the same PRD puts real Manager/Worker/Reviewer role runtime in v1 (PRD 24, line 2322-2333) and OMX/Codex as the working v0/v1 executor (PRD 23.1 line 2253 'OMX/Codex command 출력', PRD 4.6) — both delivered only as stubs. The roadmap marks these PASS rather than acknowledging them as partial within the very v0-v2 scope it claims to honor.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Roadmap framing ('not allowed to invent a smaller target after implementation', roadmap:8) presents the narrowing as disciplined. Reality: deferring v3/SaaS is faithful, but reclassifying stubbed v1 role execution and v1 executor control as PASS is post-hoc shrinking of in-scope requirements to make 'completion' reachable.
findings.recommendation: Keep the v3+/SaaS deferrals (they are correct) but reclassify v1 role execution, executor session control, and promotion routing as PARTIAL in the acceptance matrix. State plainly: v0 done, v1 scaffolded, v2 worktree mechanics done but synthesis/roles nominal.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding holds on independent reading of code + PRD + roadmap.

DEFERRAL HALF IS FAITHFUL (finding's concession is correct): PRD 3 Non-Goals genuinely defer self Agents-SDK runtime to v3+ (PRD line 161-163), SaaS/external sync (line 172-174), broad MCP (line 175-178), auto merge/push (line 165-167). FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:9 cites these correctly. No fault here.

IN-SCOPE SHRINKING IS REAL (finding's main charge confirmed):
1) v1 role execution. PRD 4.3-4.5 (lines 222-262) define Manager (restate goal, identify context, WRITE work order, synthesize, propose next action), Worker (work in scope, produce output, generate diff, run tests), Reviewer (evaluate result vs task goal, detect blocking issues, score tool-discipline/reviewability/reusability). The actual implementation does NONE of this reasoning: runRoles() (core.ts:151) runs the SAME shell command three times with only ROLE/WORKER_ID env vars differing — confirmed by the test at core.test.ts:50 using command `node -e "console.log(process.env.WORKER_ID||process.env.ROLE||'ok')"`. manager-plan.md is a static hardcoded template (core.ts:123), not produced from task/project context. writeReview() (core.ts:188) is a file-presence + exit-code checklist, not a rubric evaluation of actual outputs/diff. Yet roadmap matrix row 245 declares "v1 role execution | Manager/Worker/Reviewer ACTUALLY EXECUTE via adapter | ... | PASS", and the roadmap's own v1 Required Product Behavior (FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:108-112) demands "Role prompt templates are executed, not just written", "Manager produces plan from task/project context", "Reviewer evaluates actual outputs/diff/logs" — none of which the code satisfies. grep confirms zero PARTIAL labels anywhere in the roadmap.

2) Executor adapter. PRD 23.1 (line 2253) and 4.6 (line 267-273) put OMX/Codex as the working v0/v1 executor. The adapters never achieve real driving: ExternalCliAdapter.launch (external-cli-adapter.ts) only emits status 'supported' if hasCurrentOmxRuntime (an ambient OMX_SESSION_ID/TMUX_PANE env), else 'unproven'/'unsupported'; codex-process-bridge.ts createCodexLaunchProof reaches 'supported' only by passively attaching to a pre-existing transcript file, otherwise 'unproven'. core.ts:144 emits 'primitive_shell'/'unproven'. The HARD_COMPLETION_GATES "Real Agent Runtime Gate" was scoped to pass merely if "Start from UI launches the task adapter by default" — a bar the stub meets. Yet matrix row 246 marks Executor adapter PASS.

NUANCE (why not higher than medium, and why finding's "silently" is slightly overstated): The project is NOT uniformly silent. PRODUCT_GATE_RERUN_REPORT.md:26 candidly states v1 is "Process-backed adapter exists; not a full custom LLM runtime, which PRD places later", and core.ts:180 runtimeTruthForRun honestly self-labels runs as primitive_shell/unproven in the UI. The deception is concentrated in the FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP binary PASS matrix and DOGFOOD_REPORT.md:39 ("PASS for process-backed manager/worker/reviewer adapter behavior"), which reclassify the PRD requirement of real role reasoning down to "three processes ran with different ROLE env vars" and call it PASS. That is post-hoc shrinking of in-scope v1 capability to make completion reachable — exactly the finding's CLAIM_VS_REALITY. Severity medium is well-calibrated: real scope-integrity/honesty defect in the headline matrix, materially mitigated by candor in the rerun report and code self-labeling, with the deferral half fully faithful.

findings.title: True completion vs full PRD is ~30-35%, not 95%; PRD's stated purpose (compounding learning loop) is largely absent
findings.evidence: PRD roadmap spans v0-v4 (PRD 24, lines 2307-2373; v4 = Personal Agent OS with cross-project memory, recurring automation, eval suite). Delivered solidly: v0 file-backed record loop (task/run CRUD core.ts:107/113, collect git evidence core.ts:174, deterministic review core.ts:188, approval-gated code apply core.ts:240), real git worktrees (core.ts:125), web UI (core.ts:502/cli.ts). Absent/stubbed: real role agents, executor session control, promotion classification, skill/workflow/eval promotion, cross-project memory wiring, eval suite. The PRD's reason-for-being (PRD 2.1-2.2, 5.5: accumulate learnings into system patches) is realized only as one generic memory-record stub.
findings.claim_vs_reality: All milestone docs converge on 'PRD-scoped local v0-v2 PASS candidate, ceiling 95'. Against the PRD as written (v0-v4, learning loop central), genuine delivery is a strong v0 plus scaffolded v1/v2 plumbing — roughly one third of the product, and the lower-value third relative to the PRD's differentiating thesis.
findings.recommendation: Report completion honestly as 'v0 functionally complete; v1/v2 partially scaffolded; learning/promotion loop not yet real' and give a single-digit-to-low-double-digit percentage against the full PRD, reserving any high number strictly for the v0 record-keeping sub-scope.

verdict.reasoning: The finding's TECHNICAL facts are confirmed against source. (1) The learning loop is the PRD's stated reason-for-being: PRD line 7 one-liner centers "승격" (promotion); section 2.1 problem #5 "시스템 개선 루프가 없다"; section 5.5 "Promotion-driven learning" (PRD:446); PRD enumerates 6 promotion targets at line 1232. (2) Promotion classification is a hardcoded stub: createPromotionProposal (core.ts:195) ALWAYS emits target_type:'memory', a fixed reason, and target_path '.agent/memory/project-facts.md'; the other 5 declared target types (core.ts:27) are never produced, and there is NO applyPromotion / no promotion ever reaches 'applied' (grep confirmed empty). (3) Memory fabric is not wired into the engine: appendMemoryFact/recommendModules/validateMemoryWrite are imported ONLY by tests and harness files (runtime-architecture.test.ts, harness/m8-boundary-evidence.ts:63-65, harness/full-target-gate.ts:107); collectRun writes no memory fact. (4) No cross-project memory: memory/fabric.ts recommendModules/readMemoryFabric operate on a single agentDir, no registry aggregation. (5) No eval suite: only a static evals/rubric.md template at init (core.ts:100); no runner/cases.yaml. (6) Role agents and adapters are shell/unproven: 'roles' mode reruns the same shell command 3x with ROLE env (core.ts:151-160); codex/omx/agy adapters self-label 'unproven'/'unsupported' and never start a real LLM session (codex-adapter.ts:71, codex-process-bridge.ts:57). HOWEVER the finding's HEADLINE framing ("95% is really 30-35% vs full PRD") misrepresents what the docs claim. HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md:11,17 and FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:1,3,9,22 repeatedly and explicitly scope the 95/PASS claim to "PRD-scoped local v0-v2 completion candidate" and state v3/v4 (Agents SDK, broad MCP, cross-project memory) are intentionally outside v0-v2 — the docs never claim 95% of the full v0-v4 product. So the docs are internally honest about deferring the higher-value v3/v4 learning machinery; CLAIM_VS_REALITY's premise that "all milestone docs converge on full-product PASS" is inaccurate. The genuinely defensible sharp critique is narrower: even within the self-declared v0-v2 scope, the matrix row "Promotion proposals — PASS / real review-derived proposal lifecycle" (FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md:248) overstates a hardcoded single-target stub with no classification and no apply path.

dimensions.summary: Catastrophic version-control integrity failure on a project whose entire stated thesis is durable evidence and anti-self-deception. The committed/pushed repository (origin/main == local HEAD == d3daaeb) contains only 3 TypeScript source files (src/cli.ts, src/core.ts, src/core.test.ts); the ENTIRE runtime architecture it advertises -- src/runtime, src/harness, src/events, src/memory, src/policy, src/projection, src/composition, src/runtime-architecture.test.ts, and docs/architecture/ -- is untracked and has NEVER been committed in any branch. Worse, there are two divergent realities: the committed HEAD version of core.ts (477 lines) is a self-contained older build with no cross-tree imports, while the uncommitted working-tree version (506 lines) is the "real" product that imports 13+ untracked files. So the runtime/harness the commit messages and milestone docs describe as delivered and gated exists only as uncommitted, unpushed working-tree state. The completion-gate test suite is also flaky (95/94/93 pass across runs) with the failing test being the anti-self-deception gate itself asserting 'PASS' !== 'FAIL'. The commit narrative ("Prevent false completion claims with executable gates," "95 tests pass") is materially disconnected from committed reality.

findings.title: Entire runtime/harness/evidence architecture is untracked and never committed in any branch
findings.evidence: git ls-files src/ returns ONLY src/cli.ts, src/core.test.ts, src/core.ts (3 files). git ls-files --others --exclude-standard lists 22 untracked .ts files across src/runtime/ (9 files), src/harness/ (7), src/events/ledger.ts, src/projection/ (2), src/memory/ (2), src/policy/permission-broker.ts, src/composition/composition.ts, plus src/runtime-architecture.test.ts and docs/architecture/PHYSICAL_RUNTIME_ARCHITECTURE.md. 'git log --all --oneline -- src/runtime/ src/harness/ src/events/' returns EMPTY -- these trees have never been committed in any branch, ever. origin/main (git ls-tree -r origin/main) contains the same 3 src files, so a fresh clone gets none of the runtime.
findings.claim_vs_reality: Commit d3daaeb 'Prevent false completion claims with executable gates' and the milestone docs (HARD_COMPLETION_GATES.md, FULL_PRODUCT_ROADMAP.md) describe a delivered, gated runtime/harness with 7 hard gates PASS and a 95 completion ceiling. Reality: that machinery is entirely outside version control and absent from the remote. The 'durable evidence' product ships its central evidence machinery as ephemeral untracked files.
findings.recommendation: Stop all completion claims until the runtime/harness/events/memory/policy/projection/composition trees, src/runtime-architecture.test.ts, and docs/architecture/ are committed and pushed. Treat any gate result computed from untracked files as void. Add a CI check (and a local pre-claim gate) that fails if 'git status --porcelain' shows untracked .ts under src/ or if any committed source imports a path not present in git ls-files.

verdict.reasoning:
Every claim in the finding holds under independent verification, and the situation is materially worse than stated.

1. `git ls-files src/` returns exactly 3 tracked files: src/cli.ts, src/core.test.ts, src/core.ts.
